
---

### 4.3 Cross-Domain Pivot Attack Detection — Sequence Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker as Threat Actor
    participant IT as IT Corporate Network
    participant VPN as Corporate VPN
    participant IDMZ as Industrial DMZ
    participant HMI as OT HMI Console
    participant SIEM as Unified SIEM
    participant SOC as SOC Analyst
    participant Plant as Plant Operations

    Attacker->>IT: Step 1 Initial phishing on IT endpoint
    IT->>VPN: Step 2 Lateral movement and credential theft
    VPN->>IDMZ: Step 3 Stolen credential VPN into IDMZ jump server
    IDMZ->>HMI: Step 4 Authenticate to OT HMI from new IP
    HMI->>SIEM: Step 5 HMI session log forwarded with anomalous source IP
    SIEM->>SIEM: Step 6 L3 cross-domain correlation rule fires
    SIEM->>SOC: Step 7 High severity pivot alert raised
    SOC->>Plant: Step 8 Notify plant operator and safety officer
    Plant->>HMI: Step 9 Force HMI logout and disable jump server account
    SOC->>SIEM: Step 10 SOAR playbook opens cross-domain IR ticket
```

---

### 4.4 IT-OT Convergence Maturity Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["L1 Silos - No Coordination"] --> R2["L2 Awareness - Joint Risk Register"]
    R2 --> R3["L3 Integration - Unified SIEM"]
    R3 --> R4["L4 Optimization - Dual KPIs and SLAs"]
    R4 --> R5["L5 Adaptive - ML Anomaly Detection"]

    R1 -."gap."-> R3
    R2 -."gap."-> R4
    R3 -."gap."-> R5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
**Define IT-OT convergence in the context of Security Operations. Highlight the inversion of the CIA triad as a key driver of convergence design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

IT-OT convergence is the integration of enterprise Information Technology systems (business data, applications, enterprise networks) with Operational Technology systems (industrial control, SCADA, PLCs, physical processes) under a unified security operations framework. (1 mark)

The CIA triad inversion is a key driver: IT prioritizes **Confidentiality → Integrity → Availability**, whereas OT prioritizes **Availability → Integrity → Confidentiality**, because OT outages can cause safety incidents, environmental damage, or loss of life. (1 mark)

Consequently, converged SOC architectures must enforce **asymmetric response protocols**, **passive OT monitoring**, and **safety-aware playbooks** that respect this priority inversion. (1 mark)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
**List any three architectural differences between IT and OT security monitoring environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Active Scanning Risk:** IT environments tolerate active vulnerability scans (Nessus, Qualys), but in OT, active scanning can crash PLCs and disrupt processes — OT mandates **passive monitoring only**. (1 mark)

2. **Patch Cadence:** IT systems are patched in days to weeks; OT systems (15–30 year lifecycles, often running legacy OS) are patched in months to years, requiring **compensating controls** in the interim. (1 mark)

3. **Detection Latency:** IT tolerates hours-to-days detection latency; safety-critical OT requires **milliseconds-to-seconds** detection on safety-instrumented loops. (1 mark)

*(Alternative valid differences: protocol stack — TCP/IP vs Modbus/DNP3; tooling — EDR vs ICS-IDS; lifetime — 3–5 yrs vs 15–30 yrs.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** as a theoretical framework for IT-OT convergence. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of **Level 3.5 (Industrial DMZ)** as the convergence boundary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **five major differences** between IT and OT security monitoring priorities. For each difference, justify the corresponding **convergence design implication**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks:**

The **Purdue Reference Model** (formally ISA-95) is a six-level logical segmentation framework that defines how data and control signals flow from physical processes up to enterprise business systems. (1 mark)

**Level-wise description:**

- **Level 0 — Physical Process:** Sensors, actuators, motors, valves. Direct interface with the physical world. (0.5 marks)
- **Level 1 — Basic Control:** PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers executing deterministic control loops (typically 10–100 ms cycle). (0.5 marks)
- **Level 2 — Supervisory Control:** SCADA HMIs, alarm servers, engineering workstations that supervise Level 1. (0.5 marks)
- **Level 3 — Operations Management:** MES, Historians, OEE dashboards. The boundary zone where OT data begins to inform business decisions. (0.5 marks)
- **Level 3.5 — Industrial DMZ (IDMZ):** The **convergence boundary**. A buffer network segment that brokers all data flow between IT (Levels 4–5) and OT (Levels 0–3). It hosts unidirectional gateways, jump servers, and replication brokers. (1.5 marks)
- **Level 4 — Business Planning:** ERP, CRM, supply chain. Pure IT. (0.5 marks)
- **Level 5 — Enterprise Network:** Internet, email, cloud. Pure IT. (0.5 marks)

**Role of Level 3.5 (1 mark):**
The IDMZ enforces **trust boundary translation**. No direct TCP connection is permitted from IT to OT. Instead, IT requests data, which is **replicated** (not pulled) into the IDMZ by an OT-side broker, then handed to IT. This prevents IT-borne malware (e.g., ransomware) from pivoting directly into OT. The IDMZ is also the **only** sanctioned location for security telemetry from OT to be forwarded to the enterprise SIEM.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the **Purdue Model with Converged SOC diagram** in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks:**

| # | Difference | IT Reality | OT Reality | Convergence Design Implication |
|---|------------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **CIA Priority** | C → I → A | A → I → C | Asymmetric IR playbooks; safety overrides data-loss SLAs (1.4 marks) |
| 2 | **Asset Discovery** | Agent-based CMDB | Passive DPI required | Deploy ICS-aware passive sensors; never active scan (1.4 marks) |
| 3 | **Patch Cadence** | Days–weeks | Months–years | Compensating controls (vLAN ACL, jump-server restriction) between patch windows (1.4 marks) |
| 4 | **Protocol Stack** | TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS | Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, OPC UA | SIEM must support ICS protocol parsers; correlation rules must understand write-coil semantics (1.4 marks) |
| 5 | **Response Authority** | IT can isolate host | Plant operator must authorize | Joint IR command structure; pre-authorized OT containment playbooks (1.4 marks) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students commonly list differences **without** linking them to a **convergence design implication**. Doing so caps the answer at 4/7 marks. Always close each row with an "implication" statement.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the **IT-OT Convergence Maturity Model** in detail. Propose and justify a **Convergence Index (CI)** metric that an organization can use to measure its convergence progress. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable cross-domain incident response workflow, explain how a **unified SOC** detects and responds to a **pivot attack** (e.g., IT phishing → OT compromise). Reference a real-world incident. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks:**

The **IT-OT Convergence Maturity Model** is a 5-level framework, adapted from CMMI, that measures an organization's progression from siloed IT and OT security operations to a fully adaptive, unified cyber-physical security posture. (1 mark)

**Levels (3 marks):**

- **L1 — Initial (Silos):** IT SOC and OT SOC operate independently with no shared tools, vocabulary, or runbooks.
- **L2 — Managed (Awareness):** Joint risk register; quarterly cross-domain tabletop exercises; mutual awareness of critical assets.
- **L3 — Defined (Integration):** Unified SIEM ingests both IT and OT logs; domain-specific correlation rules deployed; shared asset inventory.
- **L4 — Quantitatively Managed (Optimization):** Domain-specific KPIs (MTTD, MTTR) tracked; OT-specific SLAs enforced; SOAR playbooks with dual branches.
- **L5 — Optimizing (Adaptive):** ML-driven anomaly detection on process values; automated cross-domain response; CI ≥ 0.70 sustained.

**Proposed Convergence Index (CI) — 3 marks:**

$$
CI = w_1 \cdot A_{shared} + w_2 \cdot A_{visibility} + w_3 \cdot A_{response} + w_4 \cdot A_{governance}
$$

Where each $A_i$ is normalized to [0, 1], weights sum to 1, and:

- $A_{shared}$ = fraction of SOC tooling shared between IT and OT
- $A_{visibility}$ = fraction of OT assets visible to enterprise SIEM
- $A_{response}$ = fraction of high-severity incidents with a documented cross-domain playbook
- $A_{governance}$ = fraction of OT change-control decisions reviewed by the CISO

**Threshold:** $CI \geq 0.70$ indicates convergence achieved. **Justification:** The 0.70 threshold is consistent with the **NIST CSF "Adaptive" Tier** rating, which similarly demands ≥ 70% integration across the five CSF functions (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover).

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks:**

**Real-World Reference (1 mark):** The **2015 Ukraine BlackEnergy attack** is a textbook pivot attack. Threat actors spear-phished IT users at three Ukrainian power distribution companies, harvested VPN credentials, pivoted through the corporate IT network, traversed the IDMZ, and remotely opened circuit breakers at 30+ substations, causing a 6-hour blackout for 225,000 customers.

**Cross-Domain Incident Response Workflow (6 marks):**

| Step | Phase | Action | IT-OT Converged Element |
|------|-------|--------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Initial Access (IT)** | Phishing email delivers macro-laden document | EDR detects macro execution; SIEM logs email + endpoint event |
| 2 | **Credential Theft (IT)** | Mimikatz dumps LSASS memory | EDR alert → SIEM L1 correlation rule fires |
| 3 | **Lateral Movement (IT)** | Attacker uses stolen credentials to access VPN | VPN log + IAM log correlated in SIEM |
| 4 | **Pivot Attempt (Cross-Domain)** | VPN session originates from new geo; same user authenticates to OT HMI jump server | **SIEM L3 cross-domain correlation rule fires** (single user, multiple domains, anomalous IP) |
| 5 | **Detection (Unified SOC)** | High-severity alert raised with safety-context enrichment | SOC analyst sees unified IT + OT timeline in single console |
| 6 | **Triage & Notification** | Safety Impact Score (SIS) computed automatically; plant operator notified within 5 minutes | Converged SOAR sends SMS + voice to plant manager + CISO |
| 7 | **Containment (OT-aware)** | Pre-authorized playbook: (a) disable VPN account, (b) revoke jump-server access, (c) freeze HMI session, (d) instruct plant to switch to manual control | Joint command — IT and OT authority both required |
| 8 | **Eradication & Recovery** | OT engineering workstation reimaged from golden image; PLC logic integrity verified via cryptographic hash; process restarted under safety officer observation | OT-aware playbooks applied |
| 9 | **Lessons Learned** | Joint post-mortem: IT team, OT team, safety officer, CISO, plant manager | Converged runbook updated; CI score recalculated |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** When asked about a "pivot attack," students often describe **only** the IT phase or **only** the OT phase. Examiners explicitly allocate 3 marks to the **cross-domain correlation step** (Step 4 above). Always include the L3 correlation rule in your answer — this is the defining feature of a converged SOC.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1 Topic: IT-OT Convergence**

- ✅ **Core definition:** IT-OT convergence = unified integration of enterprise IT and industrial OT under one security governance framework.
- ✅ **CIA inversion:** IT = C-I-A; OT = A-I-C. This single fact drives every design decision.
- ✅ **Purdue Model:** 6 levels (0–5) + Level 3.5 (Industrial DMZ) as the convergence boundary.
- ✅ **Industrial DMZ (IDMZ):** Buffer zone at Level 3.5; hosts data diodes, jump servers, replication brokers; **never** direct IT-OT connection.
- ✅ **Passive vs Active monitoring:** OT requires **passive** monitoring only; active scanning can crash PLCs.
- ✅ **Asset inventory:** IT = CMDB (agent-based); OT = passive DPI discovery (ICS protocol-aware).
- ✅ **Patch cadence:** IT = days–weeks; OT = months–years (15–30 yr asset lifetime).
- ✅ **Protocol differences:** IT = TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS; OT = Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, OPC UA, BACnet.
- ✅ **Convergence Maturity Model:** 5 levels (Silos → Awareness → Integration → Optimization → Adaptive).
- ✅ **Convergence Index (CI):** Weighted aggregate of shared tooling, visibility, response, and governance; threshold ≥ 0.70.
- ✅ **Response Asymmetry Ratio (RAR):** MTTR_IT / MTTR_OT ≥ 10 in a properly converged SOC.
- ✅ **L1/L2/L3 correlation layers:** Intra-IT, Intra-OT, **Cross-domain pivot detection** (the key differentiator).
- ✅ **Real-world cases to memorize:** *Stuxnet (2010)*, *BlackEnergy Ukraine (2015)*, *TRITON/Trisis (2017)*, *Colonial Pipeline (2021)*.
- ✅ **Standards to reference:** *IEC 62443*, *NIST SP 800-82r3*, *NIST CSF*, *NERC CIP*, *ISA-95*.
- ✅ **Asymmetric IR playbooks:** Safety overrides data-loss SLAs; plant operator + CISO dual authority.
- ✅ **Three convergence planes:** IT Telemetry Plane, OT Telemetry Plane, Unified Intelligence Plane.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark answer on this topic, **always** include: (1) a Purdue Model reference, (2) at least one real-world incident, (3) a named standard (IEC 62443 or NIST 800-82), and (4) a diagram or matrix. An answer with all four elements almost always scores 12+/14.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and techniques for unified monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT746 - SECURITY OPERATIONS ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tools and techniques for unified monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Unified Monitoring** in the context of Security Operations Analysis refers to the centralized aggregation, normalization, correlation, and visualization of security telemetry collected from heterogeneous sources (endpoints, networks, applications, cloud workloads, identity systems, and threat-intelligence feeds) through a single, integrated platform — typically a **Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** or **Extended Detection and Response (XDR)** architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *Tools and techniques for unified monitoring* sits at the heart of **Module 1 – SOC Fundamentals**. Examiners expect students to demonstrate awareness of the **plumbing (collection)**, the **logic (correlation)**, and the **presentation (dashboards)** layers of a modern SOC.

| Layer | Primary Function | Typical Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Collection (Plumbing)** | Log forwarding, traffic mirroring, API polling | Syslog, WinRM, **NetFlow**, **SPAN/TAP**, Beats, NXLog |
| **Normalization (Logic)** | Parse, enrich, map to a common schema (CEF/LEEF/ECS) | Logstash, **SPL (Splunk)**, AQL (QRadar) |
| **Correlation (Reasoning)** | Multi-event pattern matching, rule scoring, ML | Splunk ES, IBM QRadar, Elastic Security |
| **Presentation (UX)** | Dashboards, KPIs, ticketing, SOAR hand-off | Grafana, Kibana, ServiceNow, SOAR platforms |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Control Tower"

Imagine an **airport control tower** overseeing a busy international hub:

- **Radar, transponders, weather feeds, ground sensors** = your **heterogeneous log sources** (firewalls, EDR, IDS, cloud audit logs).
- **The Tower Controller** = your **SIEM correlation engine**, fusing every signal into one coherent picture.
- **The giant curved screen** = your **SOC dashboard** (Kibana/Grafana/Splunk).
- **The PA system broadcasting warnings** = your **alerting & ticketing pipeline** (PagerDuty, ServiceNow, SOAR).

Without the tower, each aircraft (event) operates blind. *Unified monitoring* is the equivalent of giving every security analyst a **God's-eye view** of the entire enterprise estate in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "unified"?** Because the alternative — *siloed* monitoring (one dashboard per firewall vendor, another per EDR) — causes **alert fatigue**, **context loss**, and **dwell-time inflation**. A unified pipeline collapses the silos.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics the Examiner Will Test

- **MTTD — Mean Time to Detect:** $\overline{T_{detect}}$
- **MTTR — Mean Time to Respond / Remediate:** $\overline{T_{respond}}$
- **EPS — Events Per Second** ingestion rate
- **Fidelity / True-Positive Rate (TPR):** $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$
- **Log Coverage %** — fraction of in-scope sources onboarded
- **Parsers / Normalization Coverage %**
- **SLA Targets (Industry Standard):** MTTD ≤ **24 h** for critical, MTTR ≤ **4 h** for P1 incidents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Unified Monitoring Pipeline

A KTU 2024 examiner expects the student to articulate the **end-to-end lifecycle** of a security event through the unified monitoring stack:

1. **Ingestion (Collection Layer)**
   - *Push model:* Agent-based forwarders (Splunk Universal Forwarder, Filebeat, Wazuh agent) ship logs over **TLS 1.2+** to the central indexer.
   - *Pull model:* SIEM pulls via APIs (AWS CloudTrail, Microsoft Graph, Okta).
   - *Passive model:* Network TAP/SPAN ports mirror packets to a **Network Packet Broker (NPB)** which feeds IDS/NDR sensors (Suricata, Zeek).

2. **Normalization (Schema Mapping)**
   - Raw events are mapped to a canonical schema. The three dominant industry schemas are:
     - **CEF (Common Event Format)** — ArcSight legacy.
     - **LEEF (Log Event Extended Format)** — IBM QRadar.
     - **ECS (Elastic Common Schema)** — Elastic Stack.
   - Example mapping: Windows Event ID 4625 → `auth.fail` field in ECS.

3. **Enrichment (Context Augmentation)**
   - Geo-IP lookup, asset criticality tags, user department (from HR feed), CVE score enrichment, threat-intel IOC matching (MISP, VirusTotal).

4. **Correlation & Detection (Reasoning Layer)**
   - **Rule-based correlation:** Boolean logic over normalized fields (e.g., `5 failed logins within 60s from same IP AND then 1 success = Brute-Force Success`).
   - **Statistical correlation:** Frequency / threshold analysis.
   - **ML / UEBA:** Anomaly detection on user/entity behavior.
   - **MITRE ATT&CK TTP mapping** — every detection is tagged with a Tactic + Technique ID.

5. **Presentation & Action (UX Layer)**
   - Dashboards, alerts, automated SOAR playbooks (e.g., isolate host via EDR API, revoke token via Okta API, open ServiceNow ticket).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024:** the syllabus explicitly lists *SIEM/SOAR integration* and *MITRE ATT&CK mapping* as outcomes. A 14-mark answer that walks the examiner through all five layers will comfortably score full marks.

## 2.2 Tool Taxonomy — What to Remember

| Category | Open-Source / Free | Commercial / Enterprise |
|---|---|---|
| **SIEM** | **ELK/Elastic Security**, **Wazuh**, OSSIM, Graylog | **Splunk Enterprise Security**, IBM **QRadar**, **Microsoft Sentinel**, Exabeam, Securonix, LogRhythm |
| **EDR / XDR** | Velociraptor, OSQuery, Wazuh HIDS | CrowdStrike Falcon, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender, Trend Micro Vision One |
| **Network Monitoring (NDR)** | **Zeek**, Suricata, Moloch | Corelight, ExtraHop, Vectra AI |
| **SOAR** | **Shuffle**, StackStorm, n8n | Splunk SOAR (Phantom), Palo Alto XSOAR, IBM Resilient, Tines |
| **Threat Intel Platform (TIP)** | **MISP**, OpenCTI | ThreatConnect, Anomali, Recorded Future |
| **Dashboarding** | **Grafana**, Kibana | Splunk Dashboards, Tableau, Power BI |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{detect,i} - T_{occur,i})$ | Average detection latency across $N$ incidents | seconds / minutes / hours |
| 2 | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{resolve,i} - T_{detect,i})$ | Average remediation latency | seconds / minutes / hours |
| 3 | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | True-Positive Rate (Detection Fidelity) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | False-Positive Rate | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | Balanced score combining precision & recall | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 6 | $Storage_{daily} = EPS \cdot \bar{L} \cdot 86400$ | Daily log volume (bytes) | bytes / day |
| 7 | $Retention_{months} = \dfrac{Quota_{GB}}{Storage_{daily} \cdot 30}$ | Hot-archive retention window | months |
| 8 | $Alert_{fatigue} = \dfrac{N_{alerts/day}}{N_{analysts}}$ | Daily alert burden per analyst | alerts / analyst / day |
| 9 | $Parsel_{cov} = \dfrac{N_{parsed}}{N_{unique\_sources}} \times 100$ | Parser coverage % | percent |
| 10 | $KRI = w_1 MTTD + w_2 MTTR + w_3(1-TPR)$ | Composite Key Risk Indicator | weighted score |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formula 6 is *the* number every SOC architect calculates before sizing an SIEM cluster. A 10,000 EPS environment with 600-byte average event size = **~518 GB/day** raw, *before* replication, indexes, and hot/warm/cold tiering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Trade-offs

- **Storage cost vs. retention:** Raw logs are cheap on object storage (S3/Blob) but expensive when *indexed and searchable*. Hot/warm/cold tiering balances cost and analyst productivity.
- **Correlation depth vs. latency:** Multi-stage, cross-source correlation gives higher fidelity but adds seconds to alert generation.
- **Open-source vs. commercial:** OSS stack (ELK + Wazuh + Shuffle) has zero license cost but high engineering effort; commercial SIEM gives vendor SLAs and pre-built content (Sigma rules, ATT&CK mappings) at licensing cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sizing a SIEM Cluster (EPS → Storage)

A mid-size e-commerce enterprise generates logs from the following sources. Compute the **daily raw storage** and the **30-day hot retention** in TB.

| Source | EPS | Avg. event size $\bar{L}$ (bytes) |
|---|---|---|
| Firewall (Palo Alto) | 4,000 | 450 |
| EDR (CrowdStrike) | 1,500 | 800 |
| Windows DC (4624/4625) | 2,000 | 350 |
| CloudTrail (AWS) | 800 | 1,200 |
| Web App (Nginx) | 6,000 | 220 |

### Step 1 — Compute per-source daily volume using $Storage_{daily} = EPS \cdot \bar{L} \cdot 86400$

**Firewall:**
$$Storage_{FW} = 4000 \times 450 \times 86400 = 1.5552 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes/day}$$

**EDR:**
$$Storage_{EDR} = 1500 \times 800 \times 86400 = 1.0368 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes/day}$$

**Windows DC:**
$$Storage_{DC} = 2000 \times 350 \times 86400 = 6.048 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes/day}$$

**CloudTrail:**
$$Storage_{CT} = 800 \times 1200 \times 86400 = 8.2944 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes/day}$$

**Nginx:**
$$Storage_{NG} = 6000 \times 220 \times 86400 = 1.14048 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes/day}$$

### Step 2 — Sum all per-source daily volumes
$$Storage_{total} = (1.5552 + 1.0368 + 0.6048 + 0.82944 + 1.14048) \times 10^{11}$$

$$Storage_{total} = 5.16672 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes/day}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to GB and TB (1 TB = $10^{12}$ bytes)
$$Storage_{total} = \dfrac{5.16672 \times 10^{11}}{10^{12}} = 0.5167 \text{ TB/day}$$

### Step 4 — Apply an index expansion factor of 3.5× (typical for Splunk/Elastic)
$$Storage_{indexed} = 0.5167 \times 3.5 = 1.8085 \text{ TB/day}$$

### Step 5 — Compute 30-day hot retention
$$Retention_{30} = 1.8085 \times 30 = 54.26 \text{ TB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** This enterprise needs roughly **55 TB of hot-tier storage** to support 30 days of searchable retention, *before* factoring in replication factor 2 for HA, taking the cluster to **~110 TB usable**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — MTTD, MTTR, and TPR Calculation

A SOC team recorded the following 5 incident timelines. Compute MTTD, MTTR, and TPR.

| Incident | $T_{occur}$ | $T_{detect}$ | $T_{resolve}$ | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I-01 | 00:00 | 01:30 | 04:00 | True Positive |
| I-02 | 02:00 | 02:10 | 03:00 | True Positive |
| I-03 | 05:00 | 14:00 | 18:00 | True Positive |
| I-04 | 10:00 | 10:15 | — | False Positive |
| I-05 | 12:00 | 13:00 | 16:00 | True Positive |

There were also **120 True Negatives** (clean events correctly classified).

### Step 1 — Compute MTTD (in minutes)
$$MTTD = \dfrac{(90)+(10)+(540)+(15)+(60)}{5} = \dfrac{715}{5} = 143 \text{ min} \approx 2\text{h }23\text{m}$$

### Step 2 — Compute MTTR (only for the 4 TPs, ignoring the FP)
$$MTTR = \dfrac{(150)+(50)+(240)+(180)}{4} = \dfrac{620}{4} = 155 \text{ min} \approx 2\text{h }35\text{m}$$

### Step 3 — Compute TPR
$$TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{4}{4+1} = 0.80 \; (80\%)$$

### Step 4 — Compute $F_1$ (assuming $Precision = 0.80$, $Recall = TPR = 0.80$)
$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.80 \times 0.80}{0.80 + 0.80} = 0.80$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The SOC is **detecting within ~2.4 hours** and **resolving within ~2.6 hours** — well inside the typical industry SLA of MTTD < 4 h and MTTR < 8 h. A 14-mark answer should always *interpret* the numbers, not just compute them.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Composite Key Risk Indicator (KRI)

Using weights $w_1 = 0.4$ (MTTD), $w_2 = 0.4$ (MTTR), $w_3 = 0.2$ (1−TPR), and the values from §3.2:

- $MTTD = 143$ min → normalize to hours: $2.38$ h
- $MTTR = 155$ min → normalize to hours: $2.58$ h
- $1 - TPR = 0.20$

$$KRI = (0.4)(2.38) + (0.4)(2.58) + (0.2)(0.20)$$

$$KRI = 0.952 + 1.032 + 0.040 = 2.024$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Lower is better.** A KRI ≥ 3.0 typically triggers a board-level "amber" rating. Our 2.024 lands in the **green** band.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Unified SOC Monitoring Stack

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional flow* of an event through a modern unified-monitoring SOC, from collection to response. All node IDs are alphanumeric, with no reserved keywords used.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Firewall Logs] --> colA[Collectors: Syslog / UF]
    srcB[EDR Telemetry] --> colA
    srcC[Cloud Audit CloudTrail] --> colB[Collectors: API Pollers]
    srcD[NetFlow TAP] --> colC[Collectors: NPB]
    srcE[Threat Intel MISP] --> enr[Enrichment Layer]
    srcF[HR Asset DB] --> enr

    colA --> norm[Normalization and Schema Mapping CEF LEEF ECS]
    colB --> norm
    colC --> norm
    norm --> enr
    enr --> idx[Hot Indexer Cluster Splunk Elastic QRadar]

    idx --> corrA[Rule Based Correlation]
    idx --> corrB[UEBA ML Anomaly]
    idx --> corrC[MITRE ATT&CK Tagger]

    corrA --> alert[Alert Queue]
    corrB --> alert
    corrC --> alert

    alert --> soar[SOAR Playbook Engine]
    alert --> dash[SOC Dashboards Kibana Grafana]
    alert --> tick[Ticketing ServiceNow Jira]

    soar --> act1[Isolate Host EDR API]
    soar --> act2[Revoke Token Okta API]
    soar --> act3[Block IP Firewall API]

    dash --> analyst[SOC Analyst L1 L2 L3]
    tick --> analyst
    analyst -.feedback.-> idx
```

## 4.2 Component Responsibility Matrix

| Block | Responsibility | Failure Mode if Absent |
|---|---|---|
| **Collectors** | Reliable transport (TLS, back-pressure) | Event loss, blind spots |
| **Normalization** | Common schema for cross-source correlation | "Tower of Babel" — alerts never correlate |
| **Enrichment** | Adds asset, identity, threat context | High-FP alerts with no triage context |
| **Indexer** | Searchable storage, retention tiers | Slow investigations, compliance gaps |
| **Correlation** | Turns 10,000 EPS into 50 meaningful alerts | Alert fatigue, missed TPs |
| **SOAR** | Automates L1 response | Analyst burnout, high MTTR |
| **Dashboard** | Real-time situational awareness | Blind management reporting |
| **Ticketing** | Audit trail, SLA tracking | Compliance & KPI failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "design a unified monitoring framework" in a 14-mark question, draw *this* layered picture and label each block with one tool of your choice. Examiners reward architectural clarity.

## 4.3 Data-Flow Sequence (Ingestion → Detection)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant FW as Firewall
    participant UF as Universal Forwarder
    participant SIEM as SIEM Indexer
    participant COR as Correlation Engine
    participant SOAR as SOAR Engine
    participant AN as SOC Analyst

    FW->>UF: Syslog over TLS 1514
    UF->>SIEM: Forward batch every 30s
    SIEM->>SIEM: Parse and normalize to ECS
    SIEM->>SIEM: Enrich with asset and geo
    SIEM->>COR: Stream matched events
    COR->>COR: Evaluate Sigma and UEBA rules
    COR->>SOAR: Trigger alert payload
    SOAR->>AN: Notify in Slack and open ticket
    AN->>SOAR: Approve isolation
    SOAR->>FW: PAN-OS API block IP
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define unified monitoring in a SOC. List any four tools used for it.**

**Model Answer:**
Unified monitoring is the centralized collection, normalization, correlation, and visualization of security telemetry from heterogeneous sources (endpoints, networks, cloud, applications) through a single integrated platform such as a SIEM or XDR.

Four tools (any four): **Splunk ES**, **IBM QRadar**, **Microsoft Sentinel**, **Elastic Security**, **Wazuh**, **Grafana**, **Zeek**, **Wazuh HIDS**, **MISP** (for threat intel).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 × 4 = 2 marks for tools.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Suggested]
**Differentiate between SIEM and SOAR with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SIEM | SOAR |
|---|---|---|
| Primary role | Detection & alerting (log correlation) | Response orchestration (automated playbooks) |
| Data focus | Historical log analysis, real-time correlation | Case management, API-driven action |
| Example | **Splunk ES**, QRadar | **Palo Alto XSOAR**, Splunk SOAR (Phantom) |

*(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for clean one-liner difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five functional layers** of a unified monitoring pipeline in a modern SOC. Mention **one tool** for each layer. (7 marks)

**(b)** A SOC ingests logs from 5 sources with the following EPS and average event sizes:
- Source 1: EPS = 8,000, $\bar{L}$ = 300 B
- Source 2: EPS = 2,500, $\bar{L}$ = 700 B
- Source 3: EPS = 1,200, $\bar{L}$ = 1,500 B
- Source 4: EPS = 3,500, $\bar{L}$ = 450 B
- Source 5: EPS = 5,000, $\bar{L}$ = 250 B

Compute the **total daily raw storage in TB** and the **60-day indexed storage in TB** assuming an index expansion factor of 3.2× and replication factor of 2. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Collection Layer** — push/pull/passive log acquisition.
   *Tool:* Splunk Universal Forwarder / Filebeat.
2. **Normalization Layer** — parse raw events into a canonical schema (CEF/LEEF/ECS).
   *Tool:* Logstash / Splunk's field extraction.
3. **Enrichment Layer** — add asset, identity, geo-IP, threat-intel context.
   *Tool:* MISP / OpenCTI / VirusTotal API.
4. **Correlation & Detection Layer** — rule-based + UEBA + MITRE ATT&CK tagging.
   *Tool:* Elastic ML / Splunk ES Correlation Searches.
5. **Presentation & Action Layer** — dashboards, ticketing, SOAR playbooks.
   *Tool:* Kibana / ServiceNow / XSOAR.

*Block diagram — refer to §4.1 of these notes.*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Per-source daily volume in bytes** using $V_i = EPS_i \cdot \bar{L}_i \cdot 86400$:

$$V_1 = 8000 \times 300 \times 86400 = 2.0736 \times 10^{11} \text{ B}$$

$$V_2 = 2500 \times 700 \times 86400 = 1.512 \times 10^{11} \text{ B}$$

$$V_3 = 1200 \times 1500 \times 86400 = 1.5552 \times 10^{11} \text{ B}$$

$$V_4 = 3500 \times 450 \times 86400 = 1.3608 \times 10^{11} \text{ B}$$

$$V_5 = 5000 \times 250 \times 86400 = 1.0800 \times 10^{11} \text{ B}$$

**Step 2 — Sum:**
$$V_{total} = (2.0736 + 1.512 + 1.5552 + 1.3608 + 1.0800) \times 10^{11}$$
$$V_{total} = 7.5816 \times 10^{11} \text{ B/day} = 0.7582 \text{ TB/day}$$

**Step 3 — Apply index expansion 3.2×:**
$$V_{indexed} = 0.7582 \times 3.2 = 2.4261 \text{ TB/day}$$

**Step 4 — 60-day indexed:**
$$V_{60} = 2.4261 \times 60 = 145.57 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 5 — Apply replication factor 2 for HA:**
$$V_{HA} = 145.57 \times 2 = 291.13 \text{ TB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently *forget* the **index expansion factor** (typically 2×–5×) and the **replication factor** (typically 2 for HA). In real SOC design, skipping these two multipliers underestimates storage by **6×–10×**, leading to a failed cluster. Always state both factors explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **role of normalization** in unified monitoring. Compare **CEF, LEEF, and ECS** schemas with one example field mapping each. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the 5 incidents in the table below, calculate the **MTTD**, **MTTR**, and the **TPR**. Interpret the results with respect to typical SOC SLAs.

| Incident | Occurred | Detected | Resolved | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I-1 | 00:00 | 00:45 | 02:30 | TP |
| I-2 | 03:00 | 03:10 | 04:00 | TP |
| I-3 | 06:00 | 09:00 | 14:00 | TP |
| I-4 | 11:00 | 11:20 | — | FP |
| I-5 | 15:00 | 16:00 | 19:00 | TP |

(Assume 150 True Negatives for the same period.) (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Role of Normalization:** Heterogeneous vendors (Palo Alto, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Windows) emit events in proprietary formats. The SIEM cannot correlate across formats. Normalization parses every event into a **canonical schema** so a single correlation rule can span every source.

**Comparison:**

| Schema | Origin | Example Field Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| **CEF (Common Event Format)** | ArcSight (Micro Focus / OpenText) | `CEF:0|Security\|Threat\|1.0\|100\|Trojan\|10\|src=10.0.0.5 dst=8.8.8.8` |
| **LEEF (Log Event Extended Format)** | IBM QRadar | `LEEF:2.0\|Microsoft\|Windows\|10\|4625\|devTime=...\|usrName=alice` |
| **ECS (Elastic Common Schema)** | Elastic | `{ "event.category":"authentication", "event.outcome":"failure", "user.name":"alice" }` |

> All three resolve Windows Event 4625 to *failed authentication*, but the *field names* differ — that's why schema-mapping via a **parser** is mandatory before correlation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Detection deltas (minutes):**
- I-1: 45
- I-2: 10
- I-3: 180
- I-4: 20
- I-5: 60

**MTTD** (all 5 incidents):
$$MTTD = \dfrac{45+10+180+20+60}{5} = \dfrac{315}{5} = 63 \text{ min} \approx 1\text{h }3\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Resolution deltas (only TPs, in minutes):**
- I-1: 105
- I-2: 50
- I-3: 300
- I-5: 180

**MTTR:**
$$MTTR = \dfrac{105+50+300+180}{4} = \dfrac{635}{4} = 158.75 \text{ min} \approx 2\text{h }39\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — TPR:**
$$TPR = \dfrac{4}{4+1} = 0.80 \;(80\%)$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- **MTTD = 63 min** → **Excellent**, comfortably under the 4-hour SLA.
- **MTTR = 158.75 min (~2.6 h)** → **Within** the 8-hour P1 SLA.
- **TPR = 80%** → **Industry-acceptable**; many SOCs target ≥ 90%. Improvement actions: tune correlation thresholds, integrate UEBA, and reduce false positives by enriching asset context.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Two common errors cost 2–3 marks each:
> 1. **Including the FP in MTTR.** A false positive is *not* an incident — exclude it. Award 0 if student averages across all 5.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Always write `minutes` or `hours` next to the number. Examiners deduct for vague answers like "63".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Unified monitoring = SIEM + log aggregation + correlation + dashboarding in ONE platform.**
- [x] Five functional layers: **Collection → Normalization → Enrichment → Correlation → Presentation/Action.**
- [x] Three dominant schemas: **CEF, LEEF, ECS** — know the differences.
- [x] Open-source stack reference: **ELK + Wazuh + Zeek + Shuffle + MISP + Grafana.**
- [x] Commercial stack reference: **Splunk ES + QRadar/Sentinel + CrowdStrike + XSOAR.**
- [x] Core formulas: **MTTD, MTTR, TPR, F1, Storage_daily, KRI** — all in the cheat sheet (§2.3).
- [x] Storage sizing rule: **$V_{day} = EPS \cdot \bar{L} \cdot 86400$**, then × index expansion (2×–5×) × replication (typically 2).
- [x] **MITRE ATT&CK tagging** is the modern KTU 2024 expectation for every detection.
- [x] Industry SLA: **MTTD ≤ 4 h (P1)**, **MTTR ≤ 8 h (P1)**.
- [x] Common *valuation traps*: forgetting index/replication factors, including FPs in MTTR, missing units, not interpreting the numerical result.
- [x] Always pair a **diagram** (Mermaid/hand-drawn block diagram) with a 14-mark answer.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "C-NE-C-P"** for the five layers: **C**ollect, **N**ormalize, **E**nrich, **C**orrelate, **P**resent. Drill it once before walking into the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incident detection in OT networks: Threat vectors and vulnerabilities in OT environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT746 - SECURITY OPERATIONS ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Incident detection in OT networks: Threat vectors and vulnerabilities in OT environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — INCIDENT DETECTION IN OT NETWORKS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Operational Technology (OT) — Formal Definition
**Operational Technology (OT)** is a category of computing and communications systems that directly monitors, controls, or physically alters the physical world through the coordination, execution, and management of industrial processes. It encompasses **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)**, **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)**, **Distributed Control Systems (DCS)**, and **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)** used in critical infrastructure sectors such as power, water, oil & gas, manufacturing, and transportation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT746 Module 1):**
> *OT networks are purpose-built computing environments that manage industrial operations where safety, availability, and real-time deterministic response take precedence over the classic IT CIA triad's confidentiality requirement.*

### 1.2 Incident Detection in OT — Formal Definition
**Incident detection in OT** is the continuous process of identifying, classifying, and correlating anomalous events, policy violations, or malicious activities that compromise the safety, integrity, availability, or confidentiality of industrial control processes. It blends **network-based detection (NIDS)**, **host-based detection (HIDS)**, **process-behavior anomaly detection**, and **safety-instrumented system (SIS) telemetry analysis**.

### 1.3 Threat Vectors in OT Environments — Formal Definition
A **threat vector** is the pathway or mechanism by which an adversary gains unauthorized access to an OT environment to disrupt, degrade, steal, manipulate, or destroy industrial assets, processes, or data. In OT, threat vectors are often *multi-stage* and *protocol-aware*, exploiting the unique characteristics of industrial protocols such as **Modbus**, **DNP3**, **PROFINET**, **EtherNet/IP**, **IEC 60870-5-104**, and **OPC UA**.

### 1.4 Vulnerabilities in OT — Formal Definition
An **OT vulnerability** is a weakness in an industrial system's design, implementation, configuration, or operating procedure that can be exploited to compromise its safety, reliability, or operational continuity. The **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** matrix, **CWE-ICS**, and **CVE** databases jointly catalog these weaknesses, scored typically using the **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS v3.1)** on a scale of **0.0 to 10.0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why OT incident detection is uniquely hard:**
> - Legacy systems with **20–30 year** operational lifecycles cannot be patched.
> - **Real-time deterministic** constraints (often **1–100 ms** cycle times) forbid heavy security agents.
> - **Air-gaps are myths** — engineering laptops, vendor modems, and remote HMIs break isolation routinely.
> - **Safety implications** of false positives (e.g., shutting down a turbine) are far costlier than in IT.

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital ICU
Imagine a **Hospital Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** as your OT network:
- The **patients on life-support** are your **PLCs, RTUs, and DCS controllers** — they must keep running 24x7.
- The **nurses monitoring vitals** are your **HMI/SCADA workstations** — they observe but should never inject medicine directly.
- The **hospital network (Wi-Fi, visitor laptops)** is your **corporate IT** — convenient but full of germs.
- The **monitors beeping alarms** are your **intrusion detection systems (IDS)** — but unlike a hospital where alarms are *vital-sign* based, in OT they must understand *physics* (temperature, pressure, RPM).
- A **visitor bringing in a virus** is your **threat vector** (USB drop, phishing, supply-chain).
- A **patient with a weakened immune system** is your **vulnerability** (unpatched Windows XP HMI, hard-coded password on a PLC).
- The **incident response team** is your **SOC analysts** — but in OT, they must be trained clinicians, not just IT technicians, or they may "kill the patient" by rebooting a controller mid-batch.

### 1.6 Core Conceptual Anchors
| Anchor | Definition | OT Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Asset** | A component of value to be protected | PLC, HMI, Engineering Workstation, Historian |
| **Threat** | Anything that can exploit a weakness | Nation-state APT, insider, ransomware crew |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness that can be exploited | Default credentials, unpatched firmware |
| **Risk** | Probability × Impact of exploitation | R = P × I (qualitative) |
| **Impact** | Consequence of a successful attack | Production halt, safety incident, environmental release |
| **Threat Vector** | Path the threat takes to reach the asset | Remote VPN, USB drop, supply chain, lateral IT-to-OT movement |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Purdue Model / ISA-95 Reference Architecture
OT networks are best understood through the **Purdue Reference Model (PRM)** — a 6-layer logical segmentation framework (Levels 0–5) that separates enterprise (IT) from operations (OT). Incident detection strategies differ radically at each layer.

| Level | Name | Typical Assets | Detection Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L5** | Enterprise Network | ERP, Email, Cloud | IT-grade NIDS, EDR |
| **L4** | Site Business Planning | MES, File Servers | IT/OT boundary DPI |
| **L3.5** | IDMZ (Demilitarized Zone) | Firewalls, Jump Servers, AV Repos | AuthN/AuthZ hardening |
| **L3** | Site Operations | Historian, HMI Servers, Engineering WS | Protocol-aware NIDS, whitelisting |
| **L2** | Supervisory Control | SCADA Servers, HMIs, EWS | Application whitelisting, USB control |
| **L1** | Basic Control | PLCs, RTUs, DCS Controllers | Process anomaly detection, SIS correlation |
| **L0** | Physical Process | Sensors, Actuators, Motors, Valves | Physics-model deviation alarms |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Most OT breaches (Stuxnet 2010, TRITON 2017, Colonial Pipeline 2021) traversed the **IT-to-OT kill chain** from Level 5 down to Level 1. Incident detection must therefore instrument **L3.5 (IDMZ)** and **L3 (Operations DMZ)** as the highest-priority choke points.

## 2.2 Threat Vector Taxonomy in OT

### 2.2.1 External Threat Vectors
1. **Nation-State APTs** — e.g., APT33 (Shamoon), APT34 (OilRig), Sandworm (Industroyer/CRASH/Override).
2. **Cybercriminal Groups** — Ransomware-as-a-Service operators (DarkSide, BlackCat/ALPHV, LockBit) targeting industrial victims.
3. **Hacktivists** — Ideologically motivated attackers (e.g., targeting pipelines, power utilities).
4. **Competitor / Corporate Espionage** — Theft of proprietary batch recipes, process IP.

### 2.2.2 Internal Threat Vectors
1. **Malicious Insiders** — Disgruntled employees, plant operators with deep process knowledge.
2. **Negligent Insiders** — Engineers attaching unauthorized USB drives (Stuxnet's exact vector).
3. **Compromised Third Parties** — Vendors, integrators, maintenance contractors (SolarWinds-style).

### 2.2.3 Technical Threat Vectors (the *How*)
- **Remote Access Pathways:** VPN, RDP, TeamViewer, jump servers, vendor modems (dial-up), exposed HMIs on the internet.
- **Physical Pathways:** USB drops, stolen laptops, rogue devices on the plant floor, malicious maintenance.
- **Supply-Chain Pathways:** Compromised firmware in PLCs, trojanized engineering software, malicious HMI updates.
- **Wireless Pathways:** 802.11 plant Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, WirelessHART, cellular gateways.
- **Lateral Movement from IT:** Phishing → IT compromise → DMZ breach → OT propagation.
- **Protocol-Native Attacks:** Modbus function code injection, DNP3 command injection, EtherNet/IP ARP poisoning, OPC UA security bypass.

## 2.3 Vulnerability Taxonomy in OT (CWE-ICS Categories)

| CWE-ICS Class | Description | OT-Specific Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **CWE-1188 (Insecure Default)** | Default credentials, open services | `admin:admin` on PLC web interface |
| **CWE-798 (Hard-coded Credentials)** | Embedded passwords in firmware | Siemens S7 hard-coded backdoor accounts |
| **CWE-200 (Info Exposure)** | Cleartext protocol traffic | Modbus TCP, DNP3 without authentication |
| **CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write)** | Memory corruption in controllers | Buffer overflow in PLC firmware |
| **CWE-306 (Missing Auth)** | Critical commands without auth | Modbus Write Single Coil without auth |
| **CWE-862 (Missing Authorization)** | Role bypass in HMI/SCADA | Operator gaining engineer privileges |
| **CWE-1259 (Improper Zeroization)** | Sensitive data lingering in memory | Process secrets in HMIs |
| **CWE-1338 (Improper Protection of Cryptographic Boundary)** | Weak crypto in industrial protocols | DES-encrypted DNP3 instead of AES-256 |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.4.1 Risk Assessment (Qualitative & Quantitative)

**Qualitative Risk Score:**

$$R = P \times I$$

where **P** ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} is the **Likelihood** and **I** ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} is the **Impact**. Risk is rated on a 5×5 matrix producing values 1–25.

**NIST 800-30 Risk Determination:**

$$Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$$

each normalized to a value in {Low, Moderate, High, Very High}.

**CVSS v3.1 Base Score (the KTU-asked scoring standard):**

$$BaseScore = round\_up(min[(Impact + Exploitability), 10])$$

where

$$Impact = 6.42 \times ISS$$

$$ISS = 1 - [(1 - Confidentiality) \times (1 - Integrity) \times (1 - Availability)]$$

$$Exploitability = 8.22 \times AttackVector \times AttackComplexity \times PrivRequired \times UserInteraction$$

The **Temporal Score** is

$$Temporal = round\_up(BaseScore \times ExploitCodeMaturity \times RemediationLevel \times ReportConfidence)$$

and the **Environmental Score** further scales by **Confidentiality Requirement (CR)**, **Integrity Requirement (IR)**, **Availability Requirement (AR)**.

### 2.4.2 Detection Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Target (OT) |
|---|---|---|
| **MTTD (Mean Time To Detect)** | $\frac{\sum (t_{detect} - t_{occur})}{N_{incidents}}$ | **< 24 hours** (Tier 1 KPI) |
| **MTTR (Mean Time To Respond)** | $\frac{\sum (t_{contain} - t_{detect})}{N_{incidents}}$ | **< 4 hours** (Tier 1 KPI) |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | **< 5%** in OT (false alarms = production halts) |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $\frac{FN}{FN + TP}$ | **< 1%** (missed incidents = safety risk) |
| **Asset Coverage** | $\frac{Assets_{monitored}}{Assets_{total}} \times 100$ | **> 95%** for safety-critical |

### 2.4.3 The ICS Kill-Chain (SANS / Dragos 7-Stage Model)

$$\text{Stage 1: Recon} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2: Initial Intrusion} \rightarrow \text{Stage 3: Foothold} \rightarrow$$
$$\text{Stage 4: Lateral Movement} \rightarrow \text{Stage 5: Discovery} \rightarrow \text{Stage 6: Collection / Staging} \rightarrow$$
$$\text{Stage 7: Exploitation / Damage}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked "at which stage is detection optimal?", always answer **Stage 4 (Lateral Movement)** — early enough to prevent process damage, late enough that the adversary has produced observable signals (port scans, SMB enumerations, abnormal SMB/RDP traffic patterns).

### 2.4.4 The Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain (IT-Flavored but Foundational)

$$\text{Recon} \rightarrow \text{Weaponization} \rightarrow \text{Delivery} \rightarrow \text{Exploitation} \rightarrow \text{Installation} \rightarrow \text{C2} \rightarrow \text{Actions on Objectives}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential Insight:** The IT Kill Chain assumes *connectivity back* to C2; ICS Kill Chain does **not** — many OT attacks (Stuxnet, TRITON) are *air-gapped* and one-shot, making **Stage 3 (Initial Intrusion via USB)** the most critical detection point on isolated networks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
These formulas and frameworks drive:
- **IEC 62443** (Industrial Automation & Control System Security) — uses **SL-A/SL-T/SL-C** Security Levels analogous to risk.
- **NIST CSF 2.0 (2024)** — Govern / Identify / Protect / Detect / Respond / Recover.
- **NERC-CIP** (North American grid) — mandatory for power utilities.
- **TSA Security Directive 1580/82-2022-01** — mandatory for US pipelines after Colonial Pipeline.
- **CERT-IN Directions 2022 (India)** — mandatory 6-hour incident reporting, directly relevant to Indian B.Tech security operations contexts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — CVSS v3.1 Base Score for an OT Vulnerability

**Scenario:** A Schneider M340 PLC has an unauthenticated Modbus TCP service that allows a remote attacker to read/write any coil, change setpoints, and force outputs. We score it under CVSS v3.1.

### Step 1: Choose Base Metrics

| Metric | Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Attack Vector (AV)** | **Network (N) = 0.85** | Remote, over industrial Ethernet |
| **Attack Complexity (AC)** | **Low (L) = 0.77** | No special conditions; Modbus TCP is stateless |
| **Privileges Required (PR)** | **None (N) = 0.85** | No authentication in Modbus TCP |
| **User Interaction (UI)** | **None (N) = 0.85** | Automated exploit |
| **Scope (S)** | **Changed (C)** | Compromise of PLC affects the physical process (changed security authority) |
| **Confidentiality (C)** | **High (H) = 0.56** | Process secrets exposed |
| **Integrity (I)** | **High (H) = 0.56** | Setpoints writable |
| **Availability (A)** | **High (H) = 0.56** | PLC can be crashed / stopped |

### Step 2: Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISS &= 1 - \bigl[(1 - C)(1 - I)(1 - A)\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \bigl[(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \bigl[(0.44)(0.44)(0.44)\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\[4pt]
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Impact

Because **Scope = Changed**, the formula uses the full Impact equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Impact &= 6.42 \times ISS \\[4pt]
&= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\[4pt]
&= 5.8727
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Exploitability Sub-Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
Exploitability &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI \\[4pt]
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\[4pt]
&= 8.22 \times 0.4724 \\[4pt]
&= 3.8831
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Base Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
Base &= round\_up\bigl(min(Impact + Exploitability,\ 10)\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= round\_up\bigl(min(5.8727 + 3.8831,\ 10)\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= round\_up\bigl(min(9.7558,\ 10)\bigr) \\[4pt]
&= round\_up(9.7558) \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{9.8 \ (Critical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Severity Ratings per CVSS v3.1:**
> - 0.1–3.9 → Low | 4.0–6.9 → Medium | 7.0–8.9 → High | 9.0–10.0 → Critical
> The PLC vulnerability above is **Critical (9.8)** — KTU students should know that any ICS CVE scoring **> 7.0** triggers a 24-hour patch/SLA window under most enterprise OT governance.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Detection Capability by Attack Stage

| Kill-Chain Stage | Adversary Action | OT-Relevant Indicators | Detection Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Recon** | Shodan scans for exposed HMIs | Spike of unique IPs hitting TCP/502 (Modbus) or TCP/44818 (EtherNet/IP) | Edge firewall, NIDS at IDMZ |
| **2. Weaponization** | Build ICS-specific malware (Industroyer) | Internal — usually invisible | Threat intel feeds (Dragos, Claroty, Nozomi) |
| **3. Delivery** | Spear-phish engineer, drop USB | Malicious .L5X file on EWS, autorun on USB | Endpoint AV, application whitelisting |
| **4. Exploitation** | Modbus illegal function code 0x2B (Encapsulated Interface) | Anomalous function code patterns | ICS protocol-aware NIDS (Claroty, Dragos, Nozomi) |
| **5. Installation** | Modify PLC ladder logic | Engineering workstation writes outside maintenance window | Ladder-logic change-detection (e.g., SEL, Dragos) |
| **6. C2** | Cobalt Strike beacon | Outbound TLS to non-corporate domains | NetFlow, EDR, DNS monitoring |
| **7. Actions on Objectives** | Open circuit breaker, change setpoint | Process telemetry deviates from model (temperature > baseline by 3σ) | Process-anomaly detection, SIS correlation |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Risk Quantification Walkthrough (5×5 Matrix)

**Asset:** Hydrogen Compressor (H₂) at a refinery.
**Threat:** Ransomware group targeting petrochemical sector.
**Vulnerability:** Engineering WS running Windows 7 SP1, no AV updates since 2019.

| Factor | Score | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Likelihood (P)** | **4 (Likely)** | Active sector targeting + obsolete OS |
| **Impact (I)** | **5 (Catastrophic)** | Possible explosion, plant-wide shutdown |
| **Inherent Risk** | $4 \times 5 = \mathbf{20}$ | "Severe" on the 5×5 matrix |
| **Control Efficacy (C)** | Mitigation: 70% | Network segmentation (L3.5 IDMZ), EDR on EWS, offline backups |
| **Residual Risk** | $20 \times (1 - 0.70) = \mathbf{6.0}$ | "Medium" — acceptable with monitoring |

$$
\begin{aligned}
Residual\_Risk &= Inherent\_Risk \times (1 - Control\_Efficacy) \\[4pt]
&= 20 \times (1 - 0.70) \\[4pt]
&= 20 \times 0.30 \\[4pt]
&= 6.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** Any residual score > 10 requires a compensating control or executive sign-off. Any residual score > 15 is *unacceptable risk* under IEC 62443 SL-3 and must be remediated or operations must be suspended.

## 3.4 IT-vs-OT Threat-Vector Comparison (The Foundational Analytical Table)

| Dimension | IT Network | OT Network |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Asset Value** | Data (PII, IP, financial) | Physical process, safety, environment |
| **Priority of CIA** | **C**onfidentiality > Integrity > Availability | **A**vailability > Integrity > Confidentiality |
| **Patch Cycle** | Days to weeks | Months to years (planned shutdown) |
| **Mean OS Lifetime** | 3–5 years | **15–30 years** |
| **Protocol Authentication** | TLS, Kerberos, OAuth | Often **none** (Modbus, DNP3 legacy) |
| **Cycle Time** | 100 ms+ | **1–10 ms** real-time |
| **Consequence of Downtime** | Lost revenue | Lost production + safety incident + possible loss of life |
| **Adversary Goal** | Data exfiltration, ransom | Process manipulation, physical damage, ransom |
| **Detection Tolerance for FP** | Moderate (5–10% FPR acceptable) | **Very low** (< 1% FPR, false alarm = shutdown) |
| **Visibility / Telemetry** | Rich (EDR, NDR, SIEM, UEBA) | Sparse (limited sensors, no agents on PLCs) |
| **Air Gap Reality** | Almost none | Often claimed, rarely real |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The reason a *generic IT SIEM* fails in OT is the rightmost column: IT-grade detections produce FP rates that operators experience as alarm-flooding, leading to alarm-fatigue, leading to *desensitization* — at which point the *real* attack is ignored.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

Sensors **S5** and **S6** are the *last-line* defenses and must use physics-aware models to avoid generating false positives.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define Operational Technology (OT) and list three key differences between IT and OT networks.**

**Model Answer:**
OT refers to the computing and communication systems used to monitor and control industrial processes, encompassing SCADA, DCS, PLCs, and associated HMIs. Three key differences between IT and OT networks are:

1. **CIA priority is reversed** — OT prioritizes **Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality**, while IT prioritizes **Confidentiality > Integrity > Availability**.
2. **System lifecycle** — IT systems have a **3–5 year** refresh cycle, while OT systems operate for **15–30 years** without major upgrades due to capital cost and process disruption.
3. **Real-time constraints** — OT systems must operate with deterministic response times of **1–100 ms**; safety interlocks cannot tolerate variable latency, whereas IT systems tolerate second-level latency.

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for the three differences, or any other defensible differences such as patching constraints, proprietary protocols, or safety implications.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What is a threat vector in an OT environment? Give two OT-specific examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A **threat vector** is the pathway through which an adversary gains unauthorized access to an OT asset or process. Two OT-specific examples are:

1. **Compromised engineering workstation via USB drop** — as observed in the **Stuxnet (2010)** attack, where a single USB drive introduced the worm into an air-gapped Iranian nuclear facility, after which it propagated via Siemens Step 7 project files to S7-300 PLCs.

2. **Exposed industrial protocol on the Internet** — Modbus TCP (port 502) and EtherNet/IP (port 44818) servers are routinely discovered via **Shodan** and targeted by adversaries for unauthorized read/write of PLC registers.

(3 marks: 1 for the formal definition + 1 each for the two valid OT examples. Marks are awarded only if the example is genuinely OT-specific — generic IT examples like "phishing email" do not earn full credit.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Complete Answer

**Q: (a) Explain the Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95) for OT network segmentation. Describe each of its six levels (Levels 0–5) and the role of the IDMZ (Level 3.5) in incident detection. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Purdue Reference Model (PRM)**, formalized under **ISA-99/IEC 62443**, segments industrial networks into six logical levels (0 through 5) plus an IDMZ at Level 3.5, providing a defense-in-depth architecture for OT environments.

**(i) Level 0 — Physical Process:** This is the physical plant itself — sensors (temperature, pressure, flow, vibration), actuators (valves, drives, motors, breakers), and the underlying chemical/physical process. OT assets here are *non-IT* (e.g., a 4–20 mA current loop). Incident detection is physics-based: process safety alarms, Safety Instrumented System (SIS) trips, and physical anomaly sensors.

**(ii) Level 1 — Basic Control:** This layer contains the **PLCs, RTUs, and DCS controllers** that close the feedback loop with Level 0. They execute deterministic control logic (ladder, function block, structured text) on cycle times of **1–100 ms**. Incident detection here uses process-anomaly detection, controller-state monitoring, and ladder-logic change detection.

**(iii) Level 2 — Supervisory Control:** This is where **SCADA servers, operator HMIs, and alarm-management consoles** reside. Operators interact with the process here. Detection tools include application whitelisting, USB-device control, and operator-action auditing.

**(iv) Level 3 — Site Operations:** Houses the **Historian (PI, Wonderware), engineering workstations, MES interfaces, and patch servers**. This is the most data-rich OT layer and the prime target for protocol-aware NIDS (Dragos, Nozomi, Claroty).

**(v) Level 3.5 — Industrial Demilitarized Zone (IDMZ):** A **buffer zone** containing firewalls, jump servers, AV update repositories, and patch relays. It is the *choke point* for all cross-domain IT-to-OT traffic. Incident detection here is the highest-leverage: any data flow between IT and OT is *forced* through this layer, so it can be DPI-inspected, whitelisted, and logged.

**(vi) Level 4 — Site Business Planning:** The **Manufacturing Execution System (MES)**, batch records, and file servers. Sits above the IDMZ.

**(vii) Level 5 — Enterprise Network:** The **corporate IT zone** — ERP (SAP), email, Internet, cloud services. Where most attacks originate.

**Role of IDMZ in Incident Detection:**
- **Authentication enforcement** — all IT-OT traffic is brokered through jump servers; no direct IT-to-OT paths exist.
- **Protocol-aware DPI** — Modbus, DNP3, OPC UA traffic is deep-inspected for malformed packets and unauthorized function codes.
- **Data diode / one-way flow** — for sensitive telemetry, a unidirectional gateway (e.g., Waterfall, Owl Cyber) ensures only outward data flow, physically preventing inbound attacks.
- **Log aggregation** — IDMZ firewalls and proxies centralize logs into the SIEM, providing the *earliest warning* of IT-to-OT kill chain progression.
- **Containment point** — if an incident is detected, IDMZ firewalls can isolate OT in seconds without disrupting process.

> **[7 marks: 1 mark per level (6 levels = 6 marks) + 1 mark for IDMZ role.]**

---

**(b) Discuss the unique challenges of incident detection in OT networks compared to IT networks. Give at least four challenges with examples from real-world incidents (Stuxnet, TRITON, Colonial Pipeline). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Challenge 1 — Legacy and Unpatchable Systems:**
Unlike IT systems that can be patched within days, OT controllers (Siemens S7-300, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix) often run firmware 15–30 years old and cannot be patched without halting the process. *Example:* Stuxnet (2010) specifically targeted Siemens S7-315 and S7-417 PLCs whose firmware was unpatched against the vulnerability because the Iranian centrifuges operated continuously for months.

**Challenge 2 — Proprietary, Unauthenticated Protocols:**
Industrial protocols (Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104) were designed in the 1970s–80s with **no native authentication or encryption**. This makes deep-packet inspection the *only* viable detection technique. *Example:* TRITON (2017) weaponized the proprietary TriStation protocol to attack Schneider Electric Triconex Safety Instrumented Systems; standard IT signatures did not match, causing years of evasion.

**Challenge 3 — False-Positive Cost Asymmetry:**
A single false positive in IT (e.g., a benign email flagged) costs seconds. A false positive in OT (e.g., a controller erroneously flagged as compromised) may force an emergency shutdown costing **crores of rupees** per day, or trigger unsafe plant conditions. *Example:* Colonial Pipeline (2021) shut down the entire US East Coast fuel pipeline *preemptively* after a DarkSide ransomware infection on a *single* IT billing system — because the operator lacked confidence in OT segmentation. The shutdown was a risk-management decision driven by detection uncertainty.

**Challenge 4 — Sparse Telemetry and Limited Visibility:**
PLCs do not support antivirus, EDR, or even logging in many cases. Detection must rely on network sensors and process physics models. *Example:* Stuxnet initially evaded detection for over a year by replaying benign process values to HMIs while secretly altering centrifuge rotor speeds, exploiting the fact that no physics-based anomaly detection existed at Natanz.

**Challenge 5 — Convergence of IT and OT (Erosion of Air Gap):**
Modern plants use vendor VPNs, remote engineering, and cloud-based analytics, dissolving the air gap. *Example:* Industroyer (2016, Ukrainian grid) propagated through corporate VPNs to reach IEC 60870-5-104 substations, causing a 1-hour outage for 225,000 customers.

**Challenge 6 — Safety Implications of Cyber Incidents:**
A failed detection in IT = data loss. A failed detection in OT = potential *loss of life*. The Stuxnet attack destroyed **~1,000 IR-6 centrifuges** at Natanz; an equivalent attack on a chemical plant or nuclear reactor would be catastrophic.

> **[7 marks: 1.5 marks per challenge × 4 challenges = 6 marks + 1 mark for synthesis/conclusion.]**

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a) Define a threat vector. With a neat diagram, classify the major threat vectors in OT environments and explain the corresponding detection opportunity for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **threat vector** is the path or method used by a threat actor to gain unauthorized access to an OT asset, with the intent of disrupting, damaging, or extracting value from the industrial process.

**Classification of OT Threat Vectors and Detection Opportunities (tabular form for answer sheet):**

| # | Threat Vector | Description | Detection Opportunity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **External Remote Pathways** | Internet-exposed VPN, RDP, TeamViewer, HMI web interfaces | **Edge NIPS, geo-blocking, Shodan alert subscriptions** |
| 2 | **Internal Lateral Movement (IT→OT)** | Phishing of IT staff, SMB/RDP pivoting to L3 | **East-West NDR, IDMZ DPI, micro-segmentation** |
| 3 | **Physical / USB Pathways** | Malicious USB dropped in plant, stolen contractor laptop | **USB whitelisting (DataLocker, etc.), physical access control, EDR on EWS** |
| 4 | **Supply-Chain Compromise** | Trojanized firmware, malicious vendor update | **Firmware hash attestation, SBOM verification, signed updates** |
| 5 | **Wireless Pathways** | Rogue Wi-Fi AP, Zigbee/WirelessHART injection | **Wireless IDS, spectrum analysis, EAP-TLS** |
| 6 | **Protocol-Native Abuse** | Modbus command injection, DNP3 replay, OPC UA bypass | **ICS protocol-aware NIDS (Dragos, Nozomi, Claroty)** |
| 7 | **Insider Threats** | Malicious/negligent operators | **UEBA on EWS, command auditing, dual-control on critical actions** |

**Reference Diagram (drawn by student on answer sheet):**

```
[External]──────┐
[Internal]──────┤
[Physical]──────┼──> [OT ASSET] <── [DETECTION SENSORS]
[Supply-Chain]──┤                         │
[Wireless]──────┤              ┌──────────┴──────────┐
[Protocol]──────┘              │ Edge NIPS | IDMZ DPI │
                               │ USB WL  |  NDR       │
                               │ SBOM    |  ICS NIDS   │
                               └──────────────────────┘
```

**Real-world mapping:**
- *Stuxnet* used vector #3 (USB).
- *Industroyer* used vector #1 (VPN) and #6 (protocol).
- *TRITON* used vector #4 (vendor remote access) and #6 (TriStation protocol).

> **[7 marks: 1 mark definition + 4 marks classification table + 1 mark diagram + 1 mark real-world mapping.]**

---

**(b) Compute the CVSS v3.1 Base Score for the following vulnerability and interpret its severity: A networked Modbus TCP gateway allows an unauthenticated remote attacker to write arbitrary setpoints to a chemical reactor's temperature controller. The vulnerability is network-exploitable, requires low attack complexity, no privileges, no user interaction, changes the scope, and impacts confidentiality (high), integrity (high), and availability (high). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assign metric values from the problem statement:**

| Metric | Value | Numeric |
|---|---|---|
| Attack Vector (AV) | Network | **0.85** |
| Attack Complexity (AC) | Low | **0.77** |
| Privileges Required (PR) | None | **0.85** |
| User Interaction (UI) | None | **0.85** |
| Scope (S) | Changed | **—** |
| Confidentiality (C) | High | **0.56** |
| Integrity (I) | High | **0.56** |
| Availability (A) | High | **0.56** |

**Step 2 — Compute ISS (Impact Sub-Score):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISS &= 1 - [(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)] \\[4pt]
&= 1 - (0.44)^3 \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.085184 \\[4pt]
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Impact (Scope = Changed, so full formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Impact &= 6.42 \times ISS \\[4pt]
&= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\[4pt]
&= 5.873
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Exploitability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Exploitability &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI \\[4pt]
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\[4pt]
&= 3.883
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Base Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BaseScore &= round\_up(min(Impact + Exploitability,\ 10)) \\[4pt]
&= round\_up(min(5.873 + 3.883,\ 10)) \\[4pt]
&= round\_up(min(9.756,\ 10)) \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{9.8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Severity Interpretation:**
- A score of **9.8** lies in the range **9.0–10.0**, classified as **CRITICAL** under CVSS v3.1.
- This represents a remotely exploitable, unauthenticated, network-reachable control-system flaw capable of manipulating a *physical* chemical process.
- Under **IEC 62443 SL-3** governance, this requires patching or compensating controls within **30 days** maximum; under **TSA Pipeline Security Directive 2022-01**, it would be a reportable finding.
- Operationally, the chemical reactor must be isolated behind a Modbus-aware firewall with read-only policy, or a unidirectional gateway must be deployed, until the vendor patch is applied.

> **[7 marks: 1 mark metrics + 2 marks ISS/Impact + 1 mark Exploitability + 1 mark BaseScore + 1 mark severity classification + 1 mark remediation mapping.]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing IT and OT threat vectors** — *phishing email* alone is **not** an OT-specific vector; you must explain how it pivots into OT (e.g., through a contractor laptop that then connects to the engineering workstation). Marks are docked for generic IT answers.
> 2. **Ignoring the IDMZ (Level 3.5)** — KTU examiners specifically test Purdue Model knowledge. Forgetting the IDMZ loses 1 mark per 7-mark sub-part.
> 3. **Forgetting "Scope = Changed" in CVSS** — many students use the *Unchanged* formula for Impact even when the vulnerability crosses a trust boundary. Always check the **S** metric first.
> 4. **Confusing CVSS v3.0 and v3.1 rounding** — the *round-up* to one decimal is mandatory; giving 9.75 instead of 9.8 loses a mark.
> 5. **Stating "Stuxnet was an IT virus"** — it was a **multi-stage OT-targeted cyber-physical weapon**; examiners expect discussion of its PLC payload (NCP 417 step 7 changes), not just its USB propagation.
> 6. **Missing real-world anchoring** — every KTU 14-mark answer should cite *at least one* real incident (Stuxnet, TRITON, Industroyer, Colonial Pipeline, BlackEnergy). Pure theoretical answers score 9–10 of 14, not 14.
> 7. **Confusing "False Positive" and "False Negative" terminology** — FP = alarm with no attack (costs shutdowns); FN = attack with no alarm (costs safety). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OT = Industrial Control Systems** (SCADA, DCS, PLC, RTU, HMI) whose primary asset is *physical process*, not data.
- **CIA priority in OT = A > I > C** (Availability first, Confidentiality last) — opposite of IT.
- **Incident detection in OT** requires **ICS protocol-aware NIDS**, **process-anomaly detection**, and **physics-based modeling** — not just IT SIEM rules.
- **Threat vectors** = *the path* (external remote, internal lateral, physical USB, supply chain, wireless, protocol-native, insider).
- **Vulnerabilities** = *the weakness* (CWE-ICS classes: default credentials, hard-coded passwords, missing auth, cleartext protocols, unpatchable firmware).
- **Purdue Model** = 6 levels (0–5) + IDMZ (3.5); L3.5 is the *choke point* for detection and containment.
- **ICS Kill Chain** (SANS/Dragos 7-stage): Recon → Initial Intrusion → Foothold → Lateral Movement → Discovery → Collection → Exploitation.
- **Optimal detection stage** = **Lateral Movement (Stage 4)** — early enough to prevent damage, late enough to be observable.
- **CVSS v3.1 Base Score = round_up(min(Impact + Exploitability, 10))**, with Impact depending on whether Scope is Unchanged or Changed.
- **Real-world anchors** you must remember: **Stuxnet 2010** (USB + PLC), **BlackEnergy 2015** (Ukrainian grid), **Industroyer 2016** (IEC 60870-5-104), **TRITON 2017** (Triconex SIS), **Colonial Pipeline 2021** (DarkSide ransomware), **Oldsmar Water 2021** (NaOH setpoint change).
- **Frameworks** to cite: **NIST CSF 2.0 (2024)**, **IEC 62443**, **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS**, **NERC-CIP**, **TSA Pipeline SD-2022-01**, **CERT-IN Directions 2022**.
- **Metrics**: MTTD target **< 24 h**, MTTR target **< 4 h**, FPR target **< 5%**, FNR target **< 1%**, asset coverage target **> 95%**.
- **Formula at a glance:**
  - $R = P \times I$ (qualitative risk)
  - $Residual = Inherent \times (1 - ControlEfficacy)$ (post-control risk)
  - $ISS = 1 - [(1-C)(1-I)(1-A)]$
  - $Exploitability = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$
  - $Base = round\_up(min(Impact + Exploit,\ 10))$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Mantra:**
> *"In OT, detection is not about finding every packet — it is about finding the **one packet that should not be there** crossing the IDMZ, the **one ladder-logic change** outside the maintenance window, and the **one process value** that defies the laws of physics."* — If you can write this in your own words in the conclusion of a 14-mark answer, you have earned the examiner's respect.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT746 - SECURITY OPERATIONS ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Detection techniques for SCADA, PLCs, and ICS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions **Industrial Control System (ICS):** An umbrella term used by KTU 2024 syllabus (aligned with NIST SP 800-82) to describe any collection of personnel, hardware, and software that actuate or monitor the behavior of industrial processes. ICS encompasses SCADA, DCS (Distributed Control Systems), PLCs, RTUs (Remote Terminal Units), and HMIs (Human-Machine Interfaces). **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition):** A centralized system that monitors and controls geographically dispersed assets (often over wide-area, low-bandwidth links). It performs supervisory control rather than direct closed-loop control, gathering telemetry from RTUs/PLCs and sending setpoint commands. **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** A ruggedized, deterministic, real-time digital computer used for direct control of industrial machinery (e.g., a robotic arm on an assembly line, a circuit breaker in a substation). It executes a user-defined logic program (typically ladder logic) in scan cycles typically of **5–50 ms**. **Detection Techniques:** A collection of passive, active, and hybrid methodologies used in Security Operations Centers (SOCs) to identify unauthorized activity, configuration drift, protocol misuse, or behavioral anomalies within Operational Technology (OT) networks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PECCT746 (Security Operations Analysis) treats *detection* as a SOC discipline — it is **not** the same as prevention. Detection = *identifying* that something bad has happened or is happening, in time to respond. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital ICU Imagine a hospital's **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)**: | Body System | Industrial Equivalent | |---|---| | Brain (conscious decisions) | SCADA Master / Control Center | | Spinal reflexes (millisecond response) | PLCs and RTUs | | Nerves (sensory signals) | Field sensors, actuators, instrumentation | | Patient monitor (passive, non-invasive) | ICS-aware IDS sensor | | Heart rate, BP, SpO₂ | Process variables (pressure, flow, temperature) | A doctor **never rewires the heart** to monitor it — she attaches sensors externally. Similarly, **ICS detection must be passive** so it never interferes with control loops. A *false positive* that shuts down a turbine is more dangerous than a *false negative*; thus, the *cost of a false alarm* in OT is far higher than in IT. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** OT detection trades off *sensitivity* for *safety*. A nuclear plant that trips offline on a false alarm may cause a regional blackout, so detection thresholds are tuned conservatively. ## 1.3 The Three Worlds of Detection - **IT (Information Technology):** Business networks, data confidentiality. - **OT (Operational Technology):** Industrial processes, physical safety. - **IoT (Internet of Things):** Consumer/embedded devices. This module focuses exclusively on **OT detection**, where the priorities are ordered: **Safety → Reliability → Availability → Integrity → Confidentiality** (the reverse of IT priorities). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Detection Pillars in ICS A robust ICS detection program rests on **four mutually reinforcing pillars**: 1. **Network-Based Detection (NIDS/NIPS for OT)** - Passive traffic capture from SPAN/TAP ports on switches/routers. - Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) of industrial protocols: *Modbus/TCP (port 502)*, *DNP3 (port 20000)*, *IEC 60870-5-104 (port 2404)*, *Profinet*, *EtherNet/IP (port 44818)*, *OPC UA*, *S7comm*. - Tools: Dragos, Nozomi Guardian, Claroty Continuous Threat Detection, Tenable.ot. 2. **Host-Based Detection (HIDS on Engineering Workstations & HMIs)** - File integrity monitoring (FIM) on engineering project files (e.g., TIA Portal `.ap15` files, RSLogix `.ACD`). - USB/removable media auditing (Stuxnet entered via USB). - Application whitelisting logs (e.g., McAfee Application Control, Windows Defender AppLocker). 3. **Process / Behavioral Anomaly Detection** - Builds a **physics-aware baseline** of the industrial process (e.g., a boiler should not exceed 250°C;
