topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT746 - SECURITY OPERATIONS ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine learning for OT-specific threat detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — MACHINE LEARNING FOR OT-SPECIFIC THREAT DETECTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Learning (ML) for OT-Specific Threat Detection** is the disciplined application of statistical learning algorithms — supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, and reinforcement-based — to Operational Technology (OT) telemetry streams (SCADA historians, PLC registers, Modbus/DNP3/IEC-60870-5-104 traffic, HMI logs, sensor time-series, and engineering workstation events) in order to infer baselines of physical-process behavior, identify deviations that signify cyber-physical attacks (false data injection, replay, command injection, denial-of-service on field devices), and classify malicious activity with a quantifiable confidence score, all while respecting the latency, determinism, and legacy-protocol constraints unique to industrial control systems (ICS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PECCT746 — Module 4):** Learners must be able to (i) characterize OT traffic, (ii) translate ICS datasets (e.g., SWaT, WADI, BATADAL, HIL-based ICS) into ML feature spaces, (iii) design a threat-detection pipeline that handles class imbalance, concept drift, and protocol-aware preprocessing, and (iv) evaluate detectors using OT-appropriate metrics (F1, MCC, detection latency, false-positive rate per shift).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cardiologist for the Plant"

Imagine a critical-care cardiologist monitoring a patient in the ICU. The patient is wired to dozens of sensors — ECG, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, respiration. The cardiologist does not memorize every possible disease; instead, she has internalized an implicit "model" of what a healthy rhythm looks like, and any subtle deviation (a skipped beat, an unusual QRS morphology) raises suspicion. She also remembers the *context* — a marathon runner's heart at 180 bpm is normal, but at rest it would be a crisis.

OT-specific ML works the same way. The **plant at steady state is the "healthy rhythm"**; the ML algorithm learns a probabilistic baseline of every actuator setpoint, valve position, tank level, and packet inter-arrival time. When a Stuxnet-style attack slowly changes motor frequencies, or a man-in-the-middle replays a stale command, the learned baseline flags the deviation *as a contextual anomaly*, not as a generic IDS rule. The crucial difference from IT-only ML is that **physics is the ground truth** — mass-balance equations, conservation laws, and process-flow dynamics are unforgiving, and a cleverly designed ML system can leverage them to detect attacks that would fool a purely signature-based IT detector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** In IT networks, ML learns from *traffic patterns*. In OT, ML learns from *physical behavior constrained by physics*. This is the central theme of Module 4 and a guaranteed high-yield question.

### 1.3 Why Traditional IT-ML Fails on OT (Foundational Premise)

| Aspect | IT Environment | OT Environment | Consequence for ML |
|---|---|---|---|
| Traffic determinism | Bursty, polymorphic | Cyclic, periodic (ms-scale) | Need temporal/sequence models |
| Asset lifetime | 3–5 years | 15–30 years | Training data is sparse and ancient |
| Confidentiality priority | Highest | Lowest (integrity + availability) | Class imbalance heavily skewed to "no attack" |
| Ground truth | Logs, NetFlow | Physics-based process variables | Semi-supervised methods are favored |
| Update tolerance | Frequent patches | Air-gapped, fragile | Online/incremental learning, not full retrain |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** Any answer that confuses IT and OT detection models (e.g., applying a spam-classifier pipeline to Modbus) will receive partial credit at best. Always stress the **physics-constrained, deterministic, and long-lifecycle** nature of OT data.

### 1.4 Taxonomy of ML Approaches for OT (Overview Map)

1. **Supervised learning** — Random Forest, XGBoost, CNN, LSTM trained on labelled attack datasets (e.g., SWaT + labelled injections).
2. **Unsupervised learning** — Autoencoders, Isolation Forest, One-Class SVM, DBSCAN trained on *normal* operation only.
3. **Semi-supervised learning** — Pseudo-labelling, label propagation; widely used because OT attack labels are rare.
4. **Reinforcement learning** — Adaptive response agents that learn optimal mitigation policies under uncertain attacker models.
5. **Deep learning** — LSTM/GRU for temporal sequences, 1-D CNN for packet payloads, Graph Neural Networks for ICS topology.
6. **Hybrid physics-informed ML** — Combines first-principles equations with neural residual learning (PINNs).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an OT-ML Detection Pipeline

A production-grade OT-ML threat detector is a six-stage pipeline. Each stage has its own KTU-relevant theory.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition & Protocol-Aware Parsing
- Passive tapping of SPAN ports on the process bus (Layer 2 mirroring), or sensor historians (PI System, Wonderware).
- Parsers convert **Modbus/TCP**, **DNP3**, **Profinet**, **EtherNet/IP**, **IEC-60870-5-104**, **OPC UA** into structured records: `function_code`, `register_address`, `coil_value`, `timestamp`, `src_ip`, `dst_ip`.
- **Whitelisting the protocol grammar** is itself a feature: any packet whose decoded fields fall outside the device's published "point list" is immediately suspect.

### Stage 2 — Feature Engineering (the heart of OT-ML)
Features fall into four families:
- **Statistical features** — mean, variance, kurtosis, skew over a sliding window $W$ of size $w$.
- **Temporal features** — inter-packet arrival time, jitter, period deviation $\Delta T_i = T_i - \bar{T}$.
- **Process features** — rate of change of tank level, mass-balance residual $r_t = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} - \dot{m}_{\text{stored}}$.
- **Topological features** — Graph adjacency from the Purdue model (Levels 0–5), used by GNNs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners' Favourite:** "Differentiate process-aware and traffic-aware features in OT-ML." Memorize the four families above.

### Stage 3 — Model Training
Choice depends on label availability (see Section 2.2).

### Stage 4 — Inference / Scoring
Each new observation $x_t$ receives an **anomaly score** $s_t \in [0,1]$ (or unbounded for some detectors). Thresholding is performed per process unit to absorb nuisance variation.

### Stage 5 — Alert Generation & SOC Triage
Alerts are correlated with **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** (T0830 — Adversary-in-the-Middle, T0855 — Unauthorized Command Message, T0836 — Modify Parameter). The ML score becomes an *evidence weight*, not a verdict.

### Stage 6 — Feedback / Continual Learning
Operator feedback (false positive / confirmed attack) is used for online updating, handling **concept drift** (e.g., seasonal setpoint changes).

---

## 2.2 The Four ML Paradigms — KTU High-Yield Synthesis

### A. Supervised Learning (when labels exist)

Given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{0,1\}$ (binary attack/no-attack) or $y_i \in \{1,\dots,K\}$ (multi-class attack family).

**Canonical models:** Random Forest (RF), eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Support Vector Machine (SVM), 1-D Convolutional Neural Network (1-D CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM).

**Decision rule:**

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in \{0,1\}} P(y=c \mid x; \theta)$$

**Training objective (empirical risk minimization):**

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\bigl(f_\theta(x_i),\, y_i\bigr) + \lambda \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $\ell_2$ penalty is essential to prevent overfitting on the small, imbalanced OT datasets (typically $N < 10^5$).

### B. Unsupervised / One-Class Anomaly Detection (the OT default)

OT labels are scarce, so the dominant paradigm is **"learn normal, flag deviations"**.

- **Isolation Forest (iForest):** Anomalies are isolated faster. The anomaly score is:

$$s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$$

where $E(h(x))$ is the average path length to isolate $x$ across $t$ random trees, and $c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$ is the average path length of an unsuccessful BST search.

- **One-Class SVM:** Learns a hypersphere enclosing normal data with margin $\rho$:

$$\min_{w,\rho,\xi} \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert w\Vert^2 + \tfrac{1}{\nu n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\xi_i - \rho \quad \text{s.t.} \; (w \cdot \Phi(x_i)) \geq \rho - \xi_i,\; \xi_i \geq 0$$

- **Autoencoder (deep):** Reconstruction error as anomaly score:

$$s(x) = \Vert x - \hat{x}\Vert_2^2 = \Vert x - g_\phi(f_\psi(x))\Vert_2^2$$

A threshold $\tau$ (e.g., 99th percentile of training residuals) yields the alarm.

### C. Semi-Supervised

Combines a small labelled set $\mathcal{D}_L$ with a large unlabelled set $\mathcal{D}_U$. Methods: pseudo-labelling, label propagation, mean-teacher architectures.

### D. Reinforcement Learning

An **agent** (the SOC) interacts with an **environment** (the OT process + adversary), observes state $s_t$ (telemetry window), chooses action $a_t$ (block IP, isolate PLC, raise alarm), and receives reward $r_t$ shaped by detection success minus disruption cost. The optimal policy $\pi^{*}(a \mid s)$ maximizes:

$$J(\pi) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi}\!\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^{t} r_t\right], \quad \gamma \in [0,1)$$

---

## 2.3 Concept Drift in OT — A Mandatory Concept

OT processes are *non-stationary*: seasonal changes (winter vs summer setpoints), maintenance reconfigurations, and equipment aging shift the input distribution $P_t(X)$. This is **concept drift** and breaks static ML.

**Page-Hinkley Test** (online drift detection):

$$m_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(x_t - \bar{x}_T - \delta), \quad M_T = \min_{1 \le t \le T} m_t, \quad \text{alarm if } m_T - M_T > \lambda_{PH}$$

**Adaptive Windowing (ADWIN):** maintains a variable-length window whose two sub-windows are tested for statistically significant mean difference. Drift is declared when:

$$\vert \mu_{W_0} - \mu_{W_1} \vert \geq \epsilon_{cut}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to mention that **drift-aware evaluation** (prequential evaluation, not hold-out) is mandatory in OT-ML.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> **Critical formatting note:** To preserve the markdown table structure, the vertical bar symbol `\|` is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` throughout.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Risk | $\hat{R}(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x_i),y_i)$ | $\mathcal{L}$: loss, $N$: sample count | Train classifier |
| 2 | L2-Regularized Risk | $R_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \hat{R}(\theta) + \lambda \Vert\theta\Vert_2^2$ | $\lambda$: penalty | Prevent overfit |
| 3 | Isolation Score | $s(x,n)=2^{-E(h(x))/c(n)}$ | $E$: mean path length, $c(n)$: BST constant | Anomaly score |
| 4 | iForest Constant | $c(n) = 2H(n-1) - \tfrac{2(n-1)}{n}$ | $H$: harmonic number | Normalization |
| 5 | One-Class SVM Primal | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert w\Vert^2 + \tfrac{1}{\nu n}\sum \xi_i - \rho$ | $\nu$: outlier fraction | One-class boundary |
| 6 | Autoencoder Score | $s(x) = \Vert x - g(f(x))\Vert_2^2$ | $f,g$: encoder/decoder | Reconstruction anomaly |
| 7 | Precision | $P = \tfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | TP/FP counts | Alarm quality |
| 8 | Recall (Detection Rate) | $R = \tfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | FN: missed attacks | Coverage |
| 9 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \tfrac{2 P R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean | Imbalance-friendly |
| 10 | MCC | $\mathrm{MCC}=\tfrac{TP\cdot TN - FP\cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | All four counts | Best for skewed data |
| 11 | Mass-Balance Residual | $r_t = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} - \dot{m}_{\text{stored}}$ | Mass flows | Physics feature |
| 12 | Period Deviation | $\Delta T_i = T_i - \bar{T}$ | $T_i$: i-th interval | Timing anomaly |
| 13 | Page-Hinkley Alarm | $m_T - M_T > \lambda_{PH}$ | Drift threshold | Drift detection |
| 14 | ADWIN Cut | $\vert \mu_{W_0} - \mu_{W_1} \vert \geq \epsilon_{\text{cut}}$ | Window means | Drift detection |
| 15 | RL Objective | $J(\pi) = \mathbb{E}\sum \gamma^t r_t$ | $\gamma$: discount | Adaptive defence |
| 16 | Softmax Probability | $P(y=c \vert x) = \tfrac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $z$: logits | Multi-class output |
| 17 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ | $y_c$: one-hot | Classifier training |
| 18 | KL Divergence | $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\Vert Q) = \sum P\log\tfrac{P}{Q}$ | Distributions | Distribution shift |
| 19 | Wasserstein-1 | $W_1(P,Q) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi}\mathbb{E}\Vert X-Y\Vert$ | Coupling $\gamma$ | Robust drift metric |
| 20 | Detection Latency | $L_d = t_{\text{alarm}} - t_{\text{attack-onset}}$ | Time difference | OT SLA metric |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of OT-ML

- **Power grids:** Real-time FDIA (False Data Injection Attack) detection on PMU streams.
- **Water treatment:** SWaT dataset benchmark — 51% attack detection F1 reached by LSTM-Autoencoder.
- **Manufacturing:** Predicting tool wear anomalies as a precursor to cyber-physical sabotage.
- **Pipeline SCADA:** Detecting slow-rate command injection that mimics operator behaviour.
- **Nuclear / Process safety:** Combining ML residuals with Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) for defence-in-depth.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Isolation Forest Anomaly Score

We rigorously derive the iForest scoring function and prove why anomalies isolate faster.

### Setup

Let $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ be the training set of "normal" OT telemetry. An Isolation Tree (iTree) is built by recursively selecting a random feature $q \in \{1,\dots,d\}$ and a random split value $p$ uniformly in the range of $q$, until each leaf contains a single point (or a tree-height limit $h_{\max} = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ is reached).

**Definition (Path Length).** The path length $h(x)$ of a point $x$ in an iTree is the number of edges traversed from the root to reach the external node containing $x$.

**Claim.** Anomalies — being few and different — are isolated in *shorter* paths because fewer random splits are required to separate them.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Average Path Length of an Unsuccessful BST Search.**

The average path length of an unsuccessful search in a Binary Search Tree with $n$ external nodes is the well-known quantity $c(n)$:

$$
c(n) = 2 H(n) - 2, \quad \text{where} \quad H(n) = \ln(n) + \gamma_E + \frac{1}{2n} - O\!\left(\frac{1}{n^2}\right)
$$

is the $n$-th harmonic number, and $\gamma_E \approx 0.5772$ is the Euler–Mascheroni constant. In compact form:

$$
c(n) = 2 \ln(n-1) + 2 \gamma_E - 2\,\frac{n-1}{n} \cdot \frac{1}{?}
$$

> *Justification.* $c(n)$ is the expected number of comparisons to find a non-existent key. For iForest we use the version with $n$ *internal* nodes, written $c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$.

**Step 2 — Expected Path Length under the Isolation Procedure.**

Since the iTree is built with random feature/split selection, the expected depth of a point $x$ — averaged over the ensemble of $t$ iTrees — is:

$$
E\!\left[h(x)\right] = \frac{1}{t}\sum_{j=1}^{t} h_j(x)
$$

**Step 3 — Normalization.**

We normalize the expected path length by $c(\psi)$ where $\psi$ is the size of the iTree's sample subsample. The normalized anomaly score is defined as:

$$
s(x,\psi) = 2^{-\frac{E[h(x)]}{c(\psi)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (anomaly behaviour).**

- If $E[h(x)] \to 0$ (point isolated at the root), then $s \to 2^{0} = 1$ → **maximum anomaly**.
- If $E[h(x)] \to c(\psi)$ (point behaves like an average BST unsuccessful search), then $s \to 2^{-1} = 0.5$ → **inconclusive**.
- If $E[h(x)] \to n-1$ (point requires near-maximal splits), then $s \to 0$ → **normal**.

**Step 5 — Detection Rule.**

Choose a contamination prior $\alpha$ (e.g., $\alpha = 0.01$ for 1% attack rate). Set threshold $\tau$ as the $\lceil (1-\alpha) n \rceil$-th smallest score. Declare:

$$
\text{alarm}(x) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & s(x,\psi) > \tau \\
0, & s(x,\psi) \le \tau
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is OT-relevant:** Isolation Forest is *unsupervised*, *fast* ($O(t \cdot \psi \cdot \log \psi)$ per query), and *unsusceptible to distance-metric pathologies* that plague k-NN in high-dimensional OT feature spaces. It is therefore the **default first-line detector** in many ICS SOC stacks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Autoencoder Reconstruction Threshold via Gaussian Tail

We derive the threshold $\tau$ for a deep autoencoder used in OT anomaly detection.

### Setup

Let $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ be a windowed OT feature vector. The autoencoder is $(f_\psi, g_\phi)$ with $f_\psi: \mathbb{R}^{d} \to \mathbb{R}^{k}$ (encoder) and $g_\phi: \mathbb{R}^{k} \to \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (decoder). The reconstruction is $\hat{x} = g_\phi(f_\psi(x))$.

The **per-sample reconstruction error** (squared $\ell_2$ residual) is:

$$
r(x) = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{d}\bigl(x_i - \hat{x}_i\bigr)^2
$$

### Assumed Error Distribution

After training on a large set of "normal" windows, the empirical distribution of $r(x)$ is well-modelled by a **chi-squared** distribution with $k$ effective degrees of freedom (i.e., the bottleneck dimensionality):

$$
r(x) \;\sim\; \sigma^2 \cdot \chi^2_{k}
$$

Therefore the survival function (probability of exceeding a value $r$) is:

$$
P(r > \tau) = 1 - F_{\chi^2_{k}}\!\left(\frac{\tau}{\sigma^2}\right)
$$

### Threshold Selection

For a target false-positive rate $\beta$ (e.g., $\beta = 10^{-3}$ per window), we set:

$$
\tau = \sigma^2 \cdot F^{-1}_{\chi^2_{k}}(1 - \beta)
$$

where $F^{-1}_{\chi^2_{k}}$ is the inverse chi-squared CDF. Detection rule:

$$
\text{alarm}(x) = \mathbf{1}\!\left[r(x) > \tau\right]
$$

### Worked Numerical Illustration

Suppose $d = 40$ process features, the autoencoder bottleneck is $k = 8$, and the empirical residual variance is $\hat{\sigma}^2 = 0.25$ after training on 50,000 normal windows. Choose $\beta = 10^{-3}$.

**Step 1.** Find the $99.9$-th percentile of $\chi^2_8$. From standard tables:

$$
F^{-1}_{\chi^2_8}(0.999) \approx 25.0
$$

**Step 2.** Compute the threshold:

$$
\tau = 0.25 \times 25.0 = 6.25
$$

**Step 3.** Interpretation. Any window whose reconstruction-error exceeds $6.25$ raises an alarm; the expected rate of false positives is $0.1\%$ of windows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners' favourite question:** "How would you set the threshold for an autoencoder in OT-ML? Justify the choice of distribution." The chi-squared answer is the *correct* one; the *common-wrong* answer is "use the 99th percentile of the training residuals" (data-driven but assumes normality of residuals — usually acceptable as engineering approximation, but chi-squared is the principled answer).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Concept-Drift Detection via ADWIN (Sketch)

ADWIN (Bifet & Gavalda) maintains a variable-length window $W$ and a test statistic on the two sub-windows $W_0$ (older) and $W_1$ (newer):

**Step 1 — Sufficient Statistics.** For each sub-window $W_j$, maintain $\mu_j$ (sample mean) and $n_j = \vert W_j \vert$ (size). Maintain also $W$ as a deque of exponentially growing buckets (a variant of the exponential histogram).

**Step 2 — Cut Detection.** Whenever the rightmost bucket's statistics become compatible — i.e., the means agree within the Hoeffding bound — collapse buckets and check:

$$
\epsilon_{\text{cut}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2 m}\ln\!\left(\tfrac{4}{\delta'}\right)}, \quad m = \tfrac{2 n_0 n_1}{n_0 + n_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Drift Alarm.** If $\vert \mu_{W_0} - \mu_{W_1} \vert \ge \epsilon_{\text{cut}}$, declare drift and *drop* the older sub-window:

$$
W \;\leftarrow\; W_1, \quad \text{trigger model retrain or adaptation}
$$

**Step 4 — False-Positive Bound.** With a confidence $\delta' \in (0,1)$, ADWIN guarantees:

$$
P(\text{false alarm}) \le \delta' \quad \text{over an infinite stream}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In an exam, sketch the test statistic and explain *why* the Hoeffding bound is the correct tool (it is distribution-free, suiting non-Gaussian OT data).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing ML Methods for OT

> **Formatting note:** As per the engine rules, the vertical bar inside cell expressions uses `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown.

| Method | Supervision | Train Cost | Inference Cost | Handles Drift | OT Suitability | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Random Forest | Supervised | Medium | $O(\log n)$ per tree | Needs retrain | High for tabular historian data | Cannot model long temporal deps |
| XGBoost | Supervised | High | $O(d \cdot d_{\text{tree}})$ | Needs retrain | High, robust to imbalance | Hyperparameter-sensitive |
| 1-D CNN | Supervised | High (GPU) | Low (real-time OK) | Needs retrain | Excellent for raw packet windows | Requires large labelled corpus |
| LSTM / GRU | Supervised/Semi | High (GPU) | Moderate | Needs retrain | Best for multivariate time-series | Slow on legacy PLCs |
| Isolation Forest | Unsupervised | $O(t \psi \log \psi)$ | $O(t \log \psi)$ | Native (re-train) | **Excellent** for first-line | Score not probabilistic |
| One-Class SVM | Unsupervised | $O(n^2)$–$O(n^3)$ | $O(d)$ per support vector | Needs retrain | Moderate | Cubic scaling, no streaming |
| Autoencoder | Unsupervised | High (GPU) | Low (real-time) | Needs retrain | Excellent for sensor fusion | Threshold choice non-trivial |
| DBSCAN | Unsupervised | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | Re-cluster | Good for protocol-level bursts | Sensitive to $\epsilon$, MinPts |
| Mean-Teacher | Semi | High | Low | Adaptive | Strong when labels scarce | Pseudo-label noise |
| DQN (RL) | Reward-based | Very high | Real-time | Native (online) | Promising for adaptive response | Reward shaping is hard |
| Physics-Informed NN | Hybrid | High | Low | Needs retrain | **Highest** trust for safety | Requires expert modelling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Heuristic (worth 2 marks):** Any answer that confuses "supervised" with "unsupervised" or that fails to mention *drift* will lose marks. Always include the drift column in your reasoning.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — End-to-End OT-ML Detection on a Synthetic SWaT-like Window

We close this section with a fully worked-out numerical example integrating the pieces above.

**Problem.** A water-treatment plant emits a window of 30 process features (tank levels L1, L2, L3, flow rates FIT101, FIT201, …, pump status P-101, P-301, valve positions MV-101, etc.). A team trains an autoencoder and an iForest on 50,000 normal windows. A new incoming window $x_{t}$ has reconstruction error $r(x_t) = 7.10$ and iForest score $s(x_t) = 0.71$. With $\tau_{\text{AE}} = 6.25$ and $\tau_{\text{iF}} = 0.65$, decide.

**Solution.**

**Step 1 — Autoencoder decision.**

$$
r(x_t) = 7.10 > \tau_{\text{AE}} = 6.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{alarm}_{\text{AE}} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Isolation Forest decision.**

$$
s(x_t) = 0.71 > \tau_{\text{iF}} = 0.65 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{alarm}_{\text{iF}} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Ensemble fusion (OR rule).**

$$
\text{alarm}_{\text{ens}} = \text{alarm}_{\text{AE}} \vee \text{alarm}_{\text{iF}} = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Mass-balance check.**

For the window, $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = 12.0$ kg/s, $\dot{m}_{\text{out}} = 11.4$ kg/s, $\dot{m}_{\text{stored}} = 0.5$ kg/s. Then:

$$
r_{\text{MB}} = 12.0 - 11.4 - 0.5 = 0.1\;\text{kg/s}
$$

The residual is within normal tolerance ($\pm 0.5$ kg/s), so the process is *physically consistent* — this suggests the anomaly is in the *cyber* layer (e.g., spoofed Modbus write to MV-101) rather than the *physical* layer.

**Step 5 — Triage output to SOC.**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Detector | AE + iForest (consensus) |
| Confidence | High (both fired) |
| Layer | Cyber (protocol/command) |
| Likely MITRE technique | T0855 — Unauthorized Command Message |
| Recommended action | Quarantine engineering workstation, snapshot PLC logic |

## 4.1 End-to-End OT-ML Threat Detection Pipeline (Functional Architecture)

> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters; no reserved keywords; all labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[OT Field Devices: PLCs RTUs Sensors] --> B1[Protocol-Aware Taps: SPAN / TAP]
    A2[Engineering Workstation / HMI] --> B1
    A3[SCADA Historian: PI Wonderware] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Parser: Modbus DNP3 IEC104 Profinet OPCUA]
    C1 --> D1[Windowing Engine: Sliding Window W of size w]
    D1 --> E1[Feature Engineering: Statistical Temporal Process Topological]
    E1 --> F1[Feature Store: Normalized and Aligned]
    F1 --> G1[Anomaly Detector 1: Isolation Forest]
    F1 --> G2[Anomaly Detector 2: Autoencoder]
    F1 --> G3[Anomaly Detector 3: One-Class SVM]
    G1 --> H1[Score Fusion: Weighted OR or Stacked]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Physics Consistency Check: Mass Balance Energy Balance]
    I1 --> J1[Alert Generator: Threshold and Severity Tag]
    J1 --> K1[SOC Triage Dashboard]
    J1 --> K2[MITRE ATT&CK for ICS Mapper: T0830 T0836 T0855]
    K1 --> L1[Operator Feedback Loop: Confirmed FP or TP]
    L1 --> M1[Continual Learning and Drift Handler: ADWIN or Page Hinkley]
    M1 --> G1
    M1 --> G2
    M1 --> G3
```

**Reading Guide.** Data flows from the OT field at the left, through parsing, windowing, feature engineering, parallel detection channels, physics-consistency filtering, alert generation, SOC triage, and operator feedback. The feedback loop closes into a drift handler that retrains the detectors — this is what makes the system *adaptive*.

## 4.2 OT Purdue Model with ML Observation Points

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P0["LEVEL 0 PROCESS"]
        P0A[Sensors and Actuators]
    end
    subgraph P1["LEVEL 1 BASIC CONTROL"]
        P1A[PLCs and RTUs]
    end
    subgraph P2["LEVEL 2 SUPERVISORY"]

    end

    end

    end

    end
    P0A --> P1A --> P2A --> P3A --> P4A --> P5A
    P1A -.Tap.- OBS1[ML Tap 1: Process Variables]

    P3A -.Tap.- OBS3[ML Tap 3: Engineering Commands]
    OBS1 --> DET[ML Threat Detection Engine]

    OBS3 --> DET
    DET --> SOC[SOC Analyst Console]
```

**Reading Guide.** Taps at Levels 1, 2, and 3 provide the multi-view telemetry that the ML engine fuses. Levels 4 and 5 are generally out of OT-ML scope (they belong to IT-ML).

## 4.3 Drift-Adaptive Retraining Loop (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Training
    Training --> Monitoring: Model Deployed
    Monitoring --> Monitoring: No Drift
    Monitoring --> DriftDetected: ADWIN or Page Hinkley Alarm
    DriftDetected --> Buffering: Collect Recent Window
    Buffering --> Retraining: Buffer Full
    Retraining --> Validation: New Model
    Validation --> Monitoring: Pass Quality Gate
    Validation --> Rollback: Fail Quality Gate
    Rollback --> Monitoring
```

**Reading Guide.** The system never stops learning; it transitions through training, monitoring, drift detection, buffering, retraining, and validation, with a rollback path on quality failure — a critical safety property for OT.

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Which ML Method to Choose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Do you have labelled attack data?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the data a temporal sequence of 100 plus steps?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Unsupervised: iForest Autoencoder OCSVM]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use LSTM or 1D CNN with Class Balancing]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Use Random Forest or XGBoost with SMOTE]
    Q3 --> Q6{Is the process safety critical?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Use Physics Informed Hybrid Autoencoder]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Use Isolation Forest as First Line then Autoencoder]
    Q4 --> SOC[Deploy with Drift Handler]
    Q5 --> SOC
    Q7 --> SOC
    Q8 --> SOC
```

**Reading Guide.** The decision tree is a quick examiner-ready reference: "labels + sequence → LSTM/CNN", "no labels → iForest/AE", "safety critical → physics-informed".

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2025]**
*Explain why traditional IT-based machine-learning intrusion-detection systems cannot be directly applied to OT environments. List any three OT-specific constraints that influence ML design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks).**

1. **Determinism and timing constraints.** OT protocols (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet) operate with millisecond periodicity; an ML pipeline that adds seconds of latency violates the real-time SLA. *(1 mark)*
2. **Lack of labelled attack data.** OT processes run for decades on the same logic; attack samples are scarce, so supervised learning is rarely feasible. Unsupervised / one-class methods are preferred. *(1 mark)*
3. **Physics-constrained behaviour.** A tank cannot instantaneously jump 10 m; this constraint can be exploited by ML (mass-balance residual features) but breaks the i.i.d. assumption of generic IT-ML. *(1 mark)*

> [!TIP]
> Other valid points: legacy/fragile equipment, air-gap assumptions, 24/7 availability, integrity/availability priority over confidentiality.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2024]**
*Define "concept drift" in the context of OT-ML. Mention one online detection test for drift.*

**Model Answer (3 marks).**

**Definition (2 marks).** Concept drift is the change over time in the joint distribution $P_t(X, Y)$ governing OT telemetry and labels. Operationally, the *input* distribution $P_t(X)$ shifts (e.g., seasonal setpoints, equipment aging) or the *posterior* $P_t(Y \mid X)$ shifts (e.g., new attack families). A static model becomes stale and its precision/recall degrade silently.

**Online test (1 mark).** The **Page-Hinkley test** computes a cumulative deviation $m_T = \sum_{t=1}^{T}(x_t - \bar{x}_T - \delta)$ and raises an alarm when $m_T - M_T > \lambda_{PH}$, where $M_T = \min_{1 \le t \le T} m_t$. Alternative: ADWIN.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2025, Module 4, Choice A]**

> **(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, describe the design of a machine-learning pipeline for OT-specific threat detection. Label each stage and explain the role of **protocol-aware parsing** and **physics-consistency filtering**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A team trains an Isolation Forest on $\psi = 256$ normal OT windows per tree, with $t = 100$ trees. For a suspicious new window $x$, the average path length across the forest is $E[h(x)] = 3.1$. Compute the anomaly score and interpret the result. The team's contamination prior is $\alpha = 0.02$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Architecture (4 marks).** Draw the six-stage pipeline (you may reproduce the mermaid diagram in SECTION_4.1): Acquisition → Parsing → Windowing → Feature Engineering → Detection (ensemble) → Triage + Drift Handler. Each stage merits a half-mark.

**Step 2 — Protocol-aware parsing (1.5 marks).** A *parser* decodes the raw OT frame into structured fields: `function_code`, `register_address`, `coil_value`, `timestamp`, `src_ip`, `dst_ip`. This step is "protocol-aware" because the parsing rules differ for Modbus/TCP (function codes 1–8, 15, 16), DNP3 (objects 30, 32, 50), IEC-60870-5-104 (ASDU type-IDs), and so on. The parser also enforces a **whitelist** of allowed register addresses per device (the "point list"), turning any out-of-list read/write into a structural anomaly. **Why it matters for ML:** it converts heterogeneous industrial protocols into a *uniform feature space* without losing semantic richness.

**Step 3 — Physics-consistency filter (1.5 marks).** After the ML detector raises an alarm, a second pass applies **first-principles equations** (mass balance, energy balance, Kirchoff's laws for electrical systems) and checks whether the proposed anomaly is *physically realizable*. If the alleged anomalous flow is impossible given the tank's geometry and pump curve, the alarm is downgraded. This filter **reduces false positives** dramatically and is a hallmark of OT-aware (vs IT-aware) ML.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute $c(\psi)$ for $\psi = 256$ (2 marks).**

$$
c(256) = 2 H(255) - \frac{2 \cdot 255}{256}
$$

Using $H(n) \approx \ln n + \gamma_E + \tfrac{1}{2n}$:

$$
H(255) \approx \ln 255 + 0.5772 + \tfrac{1}{510} \approx 5.5413 + 0.5772 + 0.00196 \approx 6.1205
$$

$$
c(256) \approx 2(6.1205) - \frac{510}{256} \approx 12.2410 - 1.9922 \approx 10.2488
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the anomaly score (2 marks).**

$$
s(x, 256) = 2^{-\frac{E[h(x)]}{c(256)}} = 2^{-\frac{3.1}{10.2488}} = 2^{-0.3025}
$$

$$
s(x, 256) = e^{-0.3025 \cdot \ln 2} = e^{-0.3025 \cdot 0.6931} = e^{-0.2097} \approx 0.811
$$

**Step 3 — Threshold and decision (1 mark).** With $\alpha = 0.02$, the threshold is the $98$th percentile of the empirical score distribution; the team reports $\tau \approx 0.62$ from training residuals.

$$
s(x) = 0.811 > \tau = 0.62 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{ALARM}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks).** The window is anomalous: its average path length is roughly *one-third* of $c(256)$, meaning only a few random splits were needed to isolate it. This is consistent with a low-density region of the OT feature space — typical of a false-data-injection or replay attack. Recommendation: cross-check with mass-balance residual and pivot to MITRE ATT&CK for ICS mapping.

---

### Question B — 14 marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2024, Module 4, Choice B]**

> **(a)** Compare **supervised, unsupervised, and semi-supervised** learning paradigms for OT threat detection. For each, give one representative model and one OT-applicable dataset. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A deep autoencoder with bottleneck $k = 10$ is trained on $N = 100{,}000$ normal OT windows. Empirical reconstruction residuals have sample variance $\hat{\sigma}^2 = 0.16$. Derive the threshold $\tau$ for a target false-positive rate of $\beta = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. A new window gives $r(x_t) = 3.8$. Decide. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Supervised (2.5 marks).** Requires labelled data $(x_i, y_i)$. Representative model: **XGBoost** (gradient-boosted decision trees) or **1-D CNN** for raw packet windows. Representative dataset: **SWaT (iTrust, Singapore)** with 946,722 labelled windows across 41 attacks. Limitation: rare attacks, label noise.

**Step 2 — Unsupervised (2.5 marks).** Trains on *normal-only* data. Representative model: **Isolation Forest** or **Deep Autoencoder**. Representative dataset: **BATADAL** (Battle of the Attack Detection Algorithms) for water-distribution SCADA. Limitation: cannot distinguish *which* attack type; only novelty.

**Step 3 — Semi-supervised (2 marks).** Combines a small labelled set with a large unlabelled set. Representative model: **Mean-Teacher** (Tarvainen & Valpola) — a student network learns from a teacher via consistency loss on unlabelled samples. Representative dataset: any ICS dataset where only a fraction of windows are labelled, e.g., HIL-based testbed logs. Limitation: pseudo-label noise propagation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Chi-squared tail model (2 marks).** Under the assumption $r \sim \sigma^2 \chi^2_k$ with $k = 10$:

$$
P(r > \tau) = 1 - F_{\chi^2_{10}}\!\left(\frac{\tau}{\sigma^2}\right) = \beta
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\tau}{\sigma^2} = F^{-1}_{\chi^2_{10}}(1 - \beta)
$$

**Step 2 — Look up the chi-squared critical value (2 marks).** For $k = 10$ and upper-tail $5 \times 10^{-4}$:

$$
F^{-1}_{\chi^2_{10}}(0.9995) \approx 29.6
$$

(Values: at $0.999$ the value is $\approx 25.19$; at $0.9995$ it is $\approx 29.6$.)

**Step 3 — Compute the threshold (1.5 marks).**

$$
\tau = \hat{\sigma}^2 \cdot 29.6 = 0.16 \cdot 29.6 = 4.736
$$

**Step 4 — Decision (1.5 marks).** New window residual $r(x_t) = 3.8$. Compare:

$$
3.8 < \tau = 4.736 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{No alarm}
$$

**Interpretation:** the new window is statistically consistent with normal behaviour under the chi-squared tail model. However, the analyst should *not* drop the window — single-window decisions are weak; accumulate evidence across a small horizon (e.g., 5 consecutive windows) and re-evaluate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places students lose marks on Module 4 OT-ML questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing IT-ML with OT-ML.** A generic answer that does not mention physics, determinism, or legacy constraints will lose 2–3 marks. Always anchor to OT context.
> 2. **Forgetting the iForest constant $c(n)$.** When asked to compute a score, students often write $2^{-h}$ without normalization, losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using accuracy on imbalanced data.** A 99.9% "all-normal" baseline trivially scores 99.9% accuracy. Always mention F1, MCC, or per-class metrics.
> 4. **Ignoring concept drift.** A static supervised model deployed for 18 months in OT will silently rot. Mention ADWIN, Page-Hinkley, or prequential evaluation.
> 5. **Skipping the threshold-derivation step.** Examiners want the *chi-squared* reasoning (or equivalent principled argument), not just "set $\tau = 0.5$". A non-derivation answer caps at 50% of the part marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **OT-ML is physics-constrained, deterministic, and long-lifecycle.** It is *not* IT-ML.
- **Three pillars:** data acquisition (protocol-aware parsing) → feature engineering (statistical + temporal + process + topological) → detection (ensemble of unsupervised methods).
- **Default detector:** **Isolation Forest** for first-line screening; **Autoencoder** for second-line deep analysis; **LSTM/1-D CNN** when labels exist.
- **Threshold for autoencoders:** chi-squared tail with $k$ = bottleneck size; set via target FPR $\beta$.
- **Threshold for iForest:** $s(x, \psi) = 2^{-E[h(x)]/c(\psi)}$; alarm if $s > \tau_{\alpha}$.
- **Concept drift is unavoidable.** Use Page-Hinkley or ADWIN; never trust hold-out evaluation alone — use *prequential* evaluation.
- **Class imbalance dominates.** Use F1, MCC, AUC-PR, not accuracy. Consider SMOTE, class-weighted losses, threshold-moving.
- **Fusion strategy matters:** OR-fusion increases recall, AND-fusion increases precision; choose per safety SLA.
- **Physics-consistency filter** (mass / energy / Kirchoff residuals) is the OT-specific secret weapon.
- **Map every alert to MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** (T0830, T0836, T0855, T0832, T0879, …) to enable SOC triage.
- **Feedback loop closes the system:** operator verdicts → drift handler → continual learning.
- **Examiners' mantra:** "Show the formula, derive the threshold, justify the metric, respect the physics."

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Word:** The single sentence that captures Module 4 is: *"In OT, machine learning does not replace physics — it listens to physics, learns its rhythm, and flags the day that rhythm breaks."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proactive security measures in OT: Threat hunting in OT networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT746 - SECURITY OPERATIONS ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Proactive security measures in OT: Threat hunting in OT networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threat Hunting in OT Networks** is the *proactive, hypothesis-driven, and intelligence-led* process of iteratively searching through Industrial Control System (ICS) / Operational Technology (OT) networks, endpoints, logs, and protocol traffic to identify **advanced persistent threats (APTs)**, malicious insiders, and stealthy adversaries that have **evaded existing automated detection controls** (firewalls, IDS/IPS, signature-based AV).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, threat hunting is positioned under **Module 4: Proactive Security Measures in OT**, alongside vulnerability management, threat intelligence, and deception technology. It complements the **NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle** (Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment, Eradication & Recovery → Post-Incident Activity) by *front-loading* the Detection & Analysis phase.

The canonical reference framework used in KTU-approved literature is:

$$\text{Hunt Maturity Model (HMM)} \in \{ HM0, HM1, HM2, HM3, HM4 \}$$

where **HM0** represents *initial (reactive)* capability and **HM4** represents *advanced (machine-augmented)* capability. The target maturity for an OT SOC is typically **HM2 (minimal) to HM3 (procedural)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Expectation):**
> - **Reactive SOC** = waits for an alert.
> - **Proactive Threat Hunting** = assumes the adversary is *already inside* the Purdue Model and searches for evidence of compromise without waiting for an alert.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large, multi-storied hospital building (the OT plant). The hospital has many security guards, CCTV cameras, and electronic door locks (these are the **firewalls, IDS/IPS, signature-based AV**).

- A **reactive approach** is when the guards only investigate *after* a fire alarm rings.
- A **proactive threat hunter** is like an experienced detective who walks through the building *every night* even when there is no alarm. He checks:
  - Whether any patient file has been secretly accessed at 3 AM (**anomalous logon time**).
  - Whether the cardiac ward's drug cabinet shows signs of tampering (**anomalous actuator command**).
  - Whether a back door that *should* be locked is found slightly ajar (**unauthorized lateral movement in Level 3**).

The detective does not wait for an alarm — he **forms a hypothesis** ("Could an attacker be using the engineering workstation as a pivot into the HMI?") and then *actively* searches for evidence to confirm or refute it.

### 1.3 Why Threat Hunting is *Critically Different* in OT vs IT

| Dimension | IT Network | OT Network |
|---|---|---|
| Primary asset | Data confidentiality | **Safety, availability, process integrity** |
| Patch cycle | Days to weeks | Months to years (Lifespan 15–25 yrs) |
| Protocols | HTTP, SMTP, SMB | **Modbus, DNP3, IEC-61850, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP** |
| Endpoint OS | Windows/Linux diverse | Mostly **Windows 7/XP Embedded, VxWorks, custom RTOS** |
| Visibility | Full packet capture possible | Often **air-gapped, partial tap points only** |
| Active scanning risk | Low | **High** — can crash controllers (PLCs, RTUs) |
| Confidentiality value | High | **Low** — value is in disruption |
| Integrity / Availability value | Medium | **Mission critical** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule in OT Threat Hunting:** **Never execute active scans, port sweeps, or fuzzing on a live Level 0–2 process network.** Use only *passive* sensor data (SPAN ports, network taps, ICS protocol-aware collectors) and historian logs.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in OT Threat Hunting

The following **standard metrics** must be committed to memory:

- **MTTD** — Mean Time To Detect (**target in OT: < 24 hours for IT/OT boundary, < 72 hours for L1-L2**)
- **MTTR** — Mean Time To Respond / Remediate
- **MTTC** — Mean Time To Contain
- **FPR** — False Positive Rate (**must be kept < 5%** in OT to avoid analyst fatigue)
- **IoC** — Indicator of Compromise
- **IoA** — Indicator of Attack (more hunting-relevant than IoC)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Threat Hunting

Threat hunting in OT is built upon **three complementary pillars**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *list and explain* these pillars.

#### Pillar A — Hypothesis-Driven Hunting
The hunter begins with an *educated hypothesis* derived from threat intelligence, a recent incident, or attacker TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures). Example hypothesis:

> *"Adversary group APT-33 has been observed abusing the Siemens S7Comm protocol to enumerate PLCs. Therefore, we will search our Level 2 network for any S7Comm packets with the `Read SZL` function code originating from non-engineering sources."*

#### Pillar B — Indicator-of-Attack (IoA) Driven Hunting
Instead of matching static indicators (IPs, hashes — which attackers change easily), the hunter searches for **behavioural patterns**:
- Anomalous ladder logic uploads during shift change.
- Engineering workstation initiating Modbus write commands to multiple slaves in < 60 s.
- HMI receiving commands from an IP outside the engineering subnet.

#### Pillar C — Situational Awareness / Baseline-Driven Hunting
The hunter knows the *normal* behaviour of every cell, zone, and conduit (per **ISA/IEC 62443 zone-conduit model**), and searches for **deviations**. This is especially powerful in OT because industrial processes are highly deterministic and repetitive.

### 2.2 Threat Hunting Frameworks (KTU High-Yield)

#### 2.2.1 The TaHiTI Methodology
**TaHiTI (Targeted Hunting integrating Threat Intelligence)** is the most commonly referenced OT threat hunting methodology in KTU-approved literature. It defines **5 iterative steps**:

1. **Trigger** — A hypothesis, IoC, or anomaly from monitoring.
2. **Hypothesis creation** — Convert the trigger into a testable statement.
3. **Investigation** — Use data sources (SIEM, historian, packet capture) to test.
4. **Findings** — Confirm benign or malicious; record artefacts.
5. **Response & Improvement** — Update detection rules, lessons learned.

#### 2.2.2 MITRE ATT&CK for ICS
The **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS Matrix** categorizes adversary behaviour against industrial systems into:
- **11 Tactics** (Initial Access, Execution, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Evasion, Discovery, Lateral Movement, Collection, Command and Control, Inhibit Response Function, Impair Process Control, Damage to Property and Equipment).
- **~80 Techniques** under these tactics.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, memorise the **11 tactics in order**. A common 3-mark question is: *"List the tactics in MITRE ATT&CK for ICS."*

#### 2.2.3 The Hunt Loop (Sqrrl / AWS Model)
A widely cited 4-stage loop:

$$\text{Hunt Loop} = \{ \text{Hypothesis} \rightarrow \text{Investigation} \rightarrow \text{Uncover} \rightarrow \text{Information Sharing} \} \rightarrow \text{repeat}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hunt Maturity (HMM)** | $\text{HMM} \in \{0,1,2,3,4\}$ | Self-assessment of SOC capability |
| 2 | **Mean Time To Detect** | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum (t_{detect} - t_{breach})}{N}$ | Lower = better; OT target ≤ 24h boundary, ≤ 72h core |
| 3 | **Mean Time To Respond** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum (t_{resolve} - t_{detect})}{N}$ | Operational efficiency metric |
| 4 | **False Positive Rate** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \times 100\%$ | Must stay below **5%** in OT |
| 5 | **Risk Score** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Impact}$ | Likelihood = (Threat × Vulnerability) |
| 6 | **Detection Coverage** | $\text{Coverage} = \frac{\text{Techniques Detected}}{\text{Total ATT\&CK-ICS Techniques}} \times 100\%$ | Map SOC detections to ATT&CK |
| 7 | **Hypothesis Confidence** | $\text{HC} = \frac{\text{Supporting Evidence Count}}{\text{Total Evidence Tested}} \times 100\%$ | Used to escalate / close hunt |
| 8 | **Asset Criticality (per IEC 62443-3-3)** | $\text{SL} \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ | Security Level target; SL-1 casual, SL-4 nation-state |
| 9 | **Zone-Conduit** | $Z = \{ \text{Assets sharing security policy} \}$ | Foundational grouping for hunt scope |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When you use `MTTD`, write the **unit explicitly** as **hours**, not minutes or days. Mixing units is a common ½-mark deduction.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Threat hunting is the *centre of gravity* of a **modern SOC** in sectors such as:
- **Power & Energy** (Smart grids, SCADA for substations).
- **Oil & Gas** (Pipeline SCADA, refinery DCS).
- **Manufacturing** (Industry 4.0, IIoT gateways).
- **Water Treatment** (Municipal SCADA — a frequent APT target).
- **Transportation** (Rail signalling, port crane automation).

The output of threat hunting directly feeds into:
1. **SIEM/IDS rule tuning** — new detection content.
2. **Incident response playbooks** — runbooks refined after each hunt.
3. **Threat intelligence platforms (TIPs)** — fresh IoCs.
4. **Risk register updates** — quantification of likelihood.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because threat hunting is a **methodology-heavy** topic rather than a calculus-heavy topic, the "derivation" here is the **exhaustive logical expansion of the TaHiTI workflow**, the **Hunt Maturity escalation logic**, and the **analytical scoring of hunt findings**. Every step is shown in full.

### 3.1 Derivation: The TaHiTI Methodology — Step-by-Step Expansion

We treat TaHiTI as a state machine and expand each state transition formally.

#### Step 1 — Trigger Generation
A hunt does not start in a vacuum. Triggers are sourced from:
- Threat intelligence feeds (STIX/TAXII).
- ATT&CK-ICS technique alert from upstream IDS.
- Asset owner report of unusual plant behaviour (e.g., a valve opening without command).

The trigger is recorded as a *hypothesis seed* $H_0$.

$$
H_0 : \text{"Adversary T0855 (Unauthorized Command Message) is active against PLC-04 in Zone-3."}
$$

#### Step 2 — Hypothesis Formalisation
The seed is converted into a *testable* hypothesis by binding it to:
- **Asset**: PLC-04 (Siemens S7-1500).
- **Protocol**: S7Comm.
- **Network location**: Purdue Level 2, Zone-3 conduit.
- **Expected evidence**: Packet with function code `0x32` (Read SZL) sourced from a non-engineering IP.

$$
H_1 : H_0 \;\wedge\; (\text{SrcIP} \notin \text{EngineeringSubnet}) \;\wedge\; (\text{FuncCode} = 0x32)
$$

> A hunter must **always** declare the negation hypothesis as well:
> $$H_{\neg} : \text{"No unauthorised SZL reads occurred in the last 30 days."}$$

This makes the hunt *falsifiable*.

#### Step 3 — Data Source Selection
Identify the *evidence sources* that can confirm or refute $H_1$:

$$
\text{DS} = \{ \text{PCAP}_\text{SPAN}, \text{SIEM Logs}, \text{Historian Audit}, \text{EWS Event Logs} \}
$$

For OT, **passive SPAN port captures on the Level 2 switch** are usually the highest-fidelity source because they preserve the wire-level protocol state.

#### Step 4 — Investigation Execution
Apply analytic queries to the data sources. A typical Structured Query Language (SQL) hunt query against the SIEM is:

```sql
SELECT timestamp, src_ip, dst_ip, s7_function_code
FROM ot_flow_logs
WHERE dst_ip = '10.20.30.44'          -- PLC-04
  AND s7_function_code = 0x32         -- Read SZL
  AND src_ip NOT IN (SELECT ip FROM engineering_workstations)
  AND timestamp > NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days';
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For OT exams, the KTU pattern is to *write the logic in plain English AND show the structural form of the query*. Do not write Python code in non-CS papers; pseudocode / structured English is preferred.

#### Step 5 — Findings Classification
Each hit is graded on a 4-point scale:

$$
\text{FindingGrade} \in \{ \text{Benign}, \text{Anomalous}, \text{Suspicious}, \text{Malicious} \}
$$

The grading is informed by:

$$
\text{Confidence}(F) = w_1 \cdot \text{IoCMatch} + w_2 \cdot \text{BehaviourAnomaly} + w_3 \cdot \text{AssetCriticality}
$$

where weights sum to 1, with **$w_3 = 0.5$** in OT (asset criticality dominates).

#### Step 6 — Response & Improvement (Closure)
Document, escalate to IR if Malicious/Suspicious, and feed new IoAs into the SIEM as detection rules. The hunt then **closes back to Step 1** with refined detection content — this is the iterative nature of threat hunting.

### 3.2 Derivation: Hunt Maturity Model (HMM) Escalation

The HMM escalates an SOC's hunting capability along five levels. Each level *subsumes* the previous.

$$
\text{HM0} \subset \text{HM1} \subset \text{HM2} \subset \text{HM3} \subset \text{HM4}
$$

**HM0 — Initial / Reactive**: SOC relies entirely on vendor alerts. No proactive search.

**HM1 — Minimal**: Ad-hoc hunts triggered by intel; manual queries. *Average dwell time > 90 days.*

**HM2 — Procedural**: Documented hunt procedures; scheduled intel-driven hunts. *Average dwell time 30–60 days.*

**HM3 — Advanced**: Hypothesis library maintained; automation of routine queries. *Dwell time 7–30 days.*

**HM4 — Leading / Machine-Augmented**: ML-assisted anomaly baselining; automated hypothesis generation. *Dwell time < 7 days.*

The current maturity of an SOC is computed as:

$$
\text{HMM}_{\text{score}} = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{4} (\text{capability present at level } i)}{5} \times 100\%
$$

> An OT SOC targeting **HM2** should score ≥ **40%** in this metric.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common OT IoCs & Their Hunt Queries

| TTP (ATT&CK-ICS) | Description | Data Source | Example Hunt Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **T0855** — Unauthorized Command Message | Unsolicited write to actuator | S7Comm/DNP3 PCAP | Write to PLC holding register from unknown master |
| **T0832** — Manipulation of View | HMI display altered | HMI audit log | Display configuration file hash change |
| **T0836** — Modify Parameter | Setpoint changed | Historian | Setpoint drift > 3σ from baseline |
| **T0858** — Change Operating Mode | PLC moved to STOP/RUN | PLC syslog | Mode change outside maintenance window |
| **T0866** — Exploitation of Remote Services | RDP/VNC into HMI | Windows Event 4624, 4648 | Logon type 10 from foreign subnet |
| **T0843** — Program Download | New ladder logic uploaded | Engineering log | Project file modification timestamp |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** An OT SOC processed 1,200 hunt alerts in a quarter. 850 were True Positives (TP), 300 were False Positives (FP), and 50 were Missed Threats (FN). True Negatives totalled 12,700. Compute: (a) FPR, (b) Detection Rate (Recall), (c) MTTD if total breach-to-detection time was 5,400 minutes across 50 incidents.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step (a) — False Positive Rate:**

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{300}{300 + 12700} = \frac{300}{13000}
$$

Computing the decimal:

$$
\frac{300}{13000} = \frac{3}{130} \approx 0.02308
$$

Converting to percentage:

$$
\text{FPR} \approx 2.31\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Below the 5% OT threshold — acceptable.

**Step (b) — Detection Rate (Recall / True Positive Rate):**

$$
\text{DR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{850}{850 + 50} = \frac{850}{900}
$$

$$
\text{DR} = 0.9444 \approx 94.44\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Strong detection — well above the 90% SOC benchmark.

**Step (c) — Mean Time To Detect (in hours):**

$$
\text{MTTD}_{\text{minutes}} = \frac{\sum (t_{detect} - t_{breach})}{N} = \frac{5400}{50} = 108 \text{ min/incident}
$$

Converting minutes → hours by dividing by **60**:

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{108}{60} = 1.8 \text{ hours/incident}
$$

**Interpretation:** Well within the KTU-target of ≤ 24 hours for IT/OT boundary.

> [!NOTE]
> This numerical problem is the **most likely 14-mark Part B pattern** for OT SOC analytics in KTU. Practice writing the units explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The diagrams below are pure **Mermaid block diagrams** using only alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase alphanumeric labels — fully compliant with the KTU rendering engine.

### 4.1 OT Threat Hunting Lifecycle (TaHiTI-aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Trigger Source TI or Anomaly] --> A2[Hypothesis H1 Falsifiable]
    A2 --> A3[Select Data Sources SPAN SIEM Historian]
    A3 --> A4[Execute Hunt Queries Passive Only]
    A4 --> A5{Grade Finding Benign Suspicious Malicious}
    A5 -->|Malicious| A6[Escalate To Incident Response]
    A5 -->|Suspicious| A7[Deep Forensics and Enrich]
    A5 -->|Benign| A8[Close Hunt Record Artefacts]
    A6 --> A9[Update Detection Rules and Playbook]
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A1
```

### 4.2 Purdue Model Mapping to Hunt Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L5[L5 ENTERPRISE ZONE]
        E1[ERP Corporate Apps]
    end
    subgraph L4[L4 SITE OPERATIONS]
        S1[Historian L4 Dashboards]
    end
    subgraph L35[L3.5 DMZ]
        D1[Jump Host OT SIEM]
    end
    subgraph L3[L3 SITE OPERATIONS CONTROL]
        C1[Engineering Workstation]
        C2[SCADA Server]
    end
    subgraph L2[L2 AREA SUPERVISORY]
        H1[HMI Stations]
        H2[Alarm Panels]
    end
    subgraph L1[L1 BASIC CONTROL]
        P1[PLC Controllers]
        P2[RTU Remote Terminal Units]
    end
    subgraph L0[L0 PROCESS]
        PR1[Sensors and Actuators]
    end
    L5 --> L4 --> L35 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1 --> L0
```

> **Hunt Scope per Zone:**
> - **L5–L3.5** → IT-style hunting (logs, EDR, network flows).
> - **L3–L2** → **OT-aware hunting (S7Comm, Modbus, DNP3 PCAP)**.
> - **L1–L0** → **Passive-only hunting via process variable anomaly baselining** (active scans forbidden).

### 4.3 MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — High-Yield Tactics Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Initial Access T0866 T0883] --> T2[Execution T0858 T0871]
    T2 --> T3[Persistence T0839 T0889]
    T3 --> T4[Evasion T0851 T0864]
    T4 --> T5[Discovery T0846 T0842]
    T5 --> T6[Lateral Movement T0867 T0880]
    T6 --> T7[Collection T0802 T0811]
    T7 --> T8[C2 T0869 T0884]
    T8 --> T9[Inhibit Response T0835 T0838]
    T9 --> T10[Impair Process T0836 T0855]
    T10 --> T11[Damage Property T0813 T0872]
```

### 4.4 Hunt Maturity Escalation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HM0[HM0 REACTIVE] --> HM1[HM1 MINIMAL]
    HM1 --> HM2[HM2 PROCEDURAL]
    HM2 --> HM3[HM3 ADVANCED]
    HM3 --> HM4[HM4 MACHINE AUGMENTED]
```

### 4.5 OT Threat Hunt Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLANT[PLANT NETWORK LEVEL 0 1 2]
        SPAN[SPAN Port Tap on L2 Switch]
        EWS[Engineering Workstation Logs]
        HIS[Process Historian Audit]
    end
    SPAN --> COL[OT Protocol Aware Collector Nozomi or Claroty or Dragos]
    EWS --> COL
    HIS --> COL
    COL --> SIEM[OT SIEM Correlator]
    SIEM --> HUNT[Hunt Workbench Analyst Console]
    SIEM --> TIP[Threat Intel Platform STIX TAXII]
    TIP --> HUNT
    HUNT --> IR[Incident Response Playbook]
    HUNT --> RULES[Detection Engineering Feedback]
    RULES --> SIEM
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define *Threat Hunting* in the context of Operational Technology networks. How does it differ from traditional *reactive* monitoring?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Threat hunting is a *proactive, hypothesis-driven* process of iteratively searching OT networks for adversaries that have bypassed existing detection controls.
- **(1 mark)** It assumes the adversary is already inside the network and searches using IoAs, threat intelligence, and baseline deviations — rather than waiting for an automated alert.
- **(1 mark)** Unlike reactive monitoring, hunting is *analyst-led*, *iterative*, and aims to reduce *dwell time*. In OT it relies on *passive* sensor data (SPAN, historian logs) because active scanning can disrupt controllers.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** List any **six** tactics of the *MITRE ATT&CK for ICS* matrix.

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per tactic, any 6):**
1. Initial Access
2. Execution
3. Persistence
4. Privilege Escalation
5. Evasion
6. Discovery
7. Lateral Movement
8. Collection
9. Command and Control
10. Inhibit Response Function
11. Impair Process Control / Damage to Property and Equipment

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **TaHiTI Threat Hunting Methodology** in detail. List its five steps and describe how each step is operationalised in an OT environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between *Indicator of Compromise (IoC)* and *Indicator of Attack (IoA)* in OT. Provide two real-world examples of each from ICS-specific incidents (e.g., TRITON, Industroyer, Havex). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TaHiTI Methodology (7 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** TaHiTI (Targeted Hunting integrating Threat Intelligence) is a structured 5-step OT threat hunting framework developed by the Dutch NCSC and Fox-IT.
- **(1 mark)** **Step 1 — Trigger:** A hunt begins with a *trigger* such as a TIP alert, ATT&CK-ICS technique detection, or asset-owner anomaly report.
- **(1 mark)** **Step 2 — Hypothesis creation:** The trigger is converted into a *falsifiable* hypothesis binding an ATT&CK technique to a specific asset, zone, and protocol. Always declare the negation hypothesis.
- **(1 mark)** **Step 3 — Investigation:** Pull data from passive SPAN taps, SIEM, historian audit logs, engineering workstation event logs. Apply structured queries.
- **(1 mark)** **Step 4 — Findings:** Grade each finding as Benign, Anomalous, Suspicious, or Malicious using a Confidence function weighted toward asset criticality.
- **(1 mark)** **Step 5 — Response & Improvement:** Escalate malicious findings, document, update detection content, refine playbooks. Loop back to Step 1.
- **(1 mark)** *OT-specific operationalisation:* Use protocol-aware collectors (Nozomi, Claroty, Dragos), avoid active scanning, integrate with historian for setpoint baselining.

**(b) IoC vs IoA in OT (7 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** **IoC** = *static, atomic artefacts* indicating past compromise (IPs, hashes, domain names, file paths). Easy for attackers to change.
- **(1 mark)** **IoA** = *behavioural, dynamic patterns* indicating an ongoing attack. More hunting-relevant because they survive adversary infrastructure changes.
- **(1 mark)** *OT IoC Example 1 (TRITON/Trisis 2017):* Known file hashes for the `Trisis.exe` TriStation protocol tool found on engineering workstations.
- **(1 mark)** *OT IoC Example 2 (Havex 2014):* C2 domains `aqq.me`, `havexhost.com` contacted by OPC scanner DLLs dropped in ICS vendors' websites.
- **(1 mark)** *OT IoA Example 1 (Industroyer 2016):* Unsolicited IEC-104 `Select and Operate` commands to a substation RTU outside the engineering schedule.
- **(1 mark)** *OT IoA Example 2 (Triton follow-on):* Engineering workstation issuing TriStation protocol `Read Status` to a Safety PLC during normal operation — should never happen.
- **(1 mark)** *Conclusion:* In OT, IoA hunting is preferred because adversary infrastructure changes rapidly, but the *physics-driven* behaviour of plant operations does not.

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Describe the **Purdue Model levels L0 to L5** and the corresponding **hunt techniques** suitable for each level. Highlight which levels demand *passive-only* hunting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An OT SOC recorded the following data for a quarter: True Positives = 900, False Positives = 200, Missed Threats = 100, True Negatives = 9,800. Compute the **FPR**, **Detection Rate (Recall)**, and **Precision**. Comment on whether the SOC meets the KTU-target FPR threshold of 5%. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Purdue Model & Hunt Techniques (7 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** **L0 — Process:** Sensors, actuators, valves. Hunting here is *process-variable baselining* (temperature, pressure anomalies). Passive only.
- **(1 mark)** **L1 — Basic Control:** PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers. Hunting via *program upload logs, mode-change events*. Passive only.
- **(1 mark)** **L2 — Area Supervisory:** HMIs, SCADA servers, alarm panels. Hunting via S7Comm / Modbus / DNP3 PCAP, HMI display hash checks.
- **(1 mark)** **L3 — Site Operations Control:** Engineering workstations, project repositories. Hunting via EDR logs, project-file diffs.
- **(1 mark)** **L3.5 — IDMZ:** Jump hosts, OT SIEM. Hunting via jump-host logon forensics and DMZ traffic flow analysis.
- **(1 mark)** **L4 — Site Operations:** Historian, business dashboards. Hunting via SQL injection patterns, historian write anomalies.
- **(1 mark)** **L5 — Enterprise:** ERP, email, IT. Standard IT hunting (EDR, NDR, UEBA). *Levels L0–L1 mandate passive-only hunting; active scanning is forbidden to avoid controller crashes.*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** TP = 900, FP = 200, FN = 100, TN = 9,800.

- **(2 marks)** **False Positive Rate:**
  $$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{200}{200 + 9800} = \frac{200}{10000} = 0.02 = 2\%$$
- **(2 marks)** **Detection Rate (Recall):**
  $$\text{DR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{900}{900 + 100} = \frac{900}{1000} = 0.90 = 90\%$$
- **(2 marks)** **Precision:**
  $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{900}{900 + 200} = \frac{900}{1100} = 0.8182 \approx 81.82\%$$
- **(1 mark)** **Conclusion / Comment:** FPR = 2% is **below the 5% KTU target** → the SOC meets the threshold. Detection Rate of 90% is acceptable but indicates 10% of true threats are still being missed, warranting detection engineering investment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** on MTTD — write **hours** explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up FPR with FDR** (False Discovery Rate = FP / (FP+TP)) — these are *different* ratios.
> 3. **Active scanning in OT** — students who write "run a Nessus scan on Level 2 PLCs" will lose **2 marks** outright. Always specify *passive* collectors.
> 4. **Confusing TTP with IoC** — TTPs are *behavioural patterns*; IoCs are *atomic artefacts*.
> 5. **Skipping the negation hypothesis** in TaHiTI — examiners specifically test for falsifiability (½ mark).
> 6. **Writing Python code** in non-CS elective papers — strictly avoid.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Threat hunting = proactive, hypothesis-driven search for adversaries that bypassed automated detection.
- **Three Pillars:** Hypothesis-driven, IoA-driven, Baseline/situational-awareness-driven.
- **Five Frameworks to Know:** TaHiTI (5 steps), MITRE ATT&CK for ICS (11 tactics), Sqrrl/AWS Hunt Loop (4 stages), HMM (5 levels), NIST SP 800-61 IR lifecycle.
- **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — 11 Tactics in order:** Initial Access → Execution → Persistence → Privilege Escalation → Evasion → Discovery → Lateral Movement → Collection → Command and Control → Inhibit Response Function → Impair Process Control / Damage to Property and Equipment.
- **TaHiTI — 5 Steps:** Trigger → Hypothesis → Investigation → Findings → Response & Improvement.
- **HMM — 5 Levels:** HM0 (Reactive) → HM1 (Minimal) → HM2 (Procedural) → HM3 (Advanced) → HM4 (Machine-Augmented). OT SOC target = HM2.
- **Purdue Mapping:** L0–L1 (process/basic control) → passive-only hunting. L2–L3 (supervisory/control) → protocol-aware PCAP hunting. L4–L5 → standard IT hunting.
- **Key Metrics:** MTTD (≤ 24h boundary, ≤ 72h core), MTTR, MTTC, FPR (≤ 5%), Detection Rate (≥ 90%), Precision.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$
  - $\text{DR / Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$
  - $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$
  - $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{\sum(t_{detect} - t_{breach})}{N}$
- **Golden Rule:** *Never active-scan Level 0–2 networks.* Use passive SPAN taps, ICS-aware collectors, historian baselines.
- **IoC vs IoA:** IoC = static artefacts (easy to change). IoA = behavioural patterns (preferred for hunting).
- **Case Studies to Memorise:** TRITON (Safety PLC targeting, 2017), Industroyer (IEC-104 substation, 2016), Havex (OPC scanner DLL in vendor websites, 2014), Stuxnet (centrifuge PLC reprogramming, 2010).
- **Output of Hunting:** Updated SIEM rules, refined IR playbooks, fresh IoCs to TIP, updated risk register.
- **Examiner Traps:** Unit-less MTTD, mixing FPR/FDR, recommending Nessus on OT, skipping negation hypothesis.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Red and purple teaming in industrial settings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT746 - SECURITY OPERATIONS ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Red and purple teaming in industrial settings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Red Teaming** is a structured, goal-oriented, full-scope adversarial simulation exercise in which an independent team of security professionals (the *red team*) emulates the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) of real-world threat actors to evaluate the effectiveness of an organization's people, processes, and technology — without prior insider knowledge of the defensive infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Purple Teaming** is a collaborative, intelligence-driven security exercise model in which the *red team* (offensive) and *blue team* (defensive) operate jointly — often in real time — to maximize organizational learning, validate detection/response capabilities, and continuously improve the defensive posture of the environment.

In **industrial settings**, these exercises are extended into the **Operational Technology (OT)** domain, encompassing **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)**, **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** networks, **Distributed Control Systems (DCS)**, and **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)** that govern physical processes in sectors such as energy, manufacturing, water treatment, and oil & gas.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bank Heist Drill Analogy**
>
> - **Red Team** = A hired team of ethical "bank robbers" who actually try to break into the vault, crack safes, bypass alarms, and steal mock valuables — but never actually take real money. They expose every weakness.
> - **Blue Team** = The bank's security guards, CCTV operators, and alarm responders who try to catch the robbers in the act.
> - **Purple Team** = A coaching mode where the robbers and guards sit at the same table after each drill. The robber explains *how* they got past the motion sensor, and the guard immediately fixes it. The next drill, the robber has to try a different way.
>
> In a **factory** (industrial setting), the vault is the **PLC controlling a chemical reactor**, the alarms are the **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** sensors, and the guards are the **SOC (Security Operations Center)** analysts. Red and purple teaming ensure that nobody actually blows up the reactor — they just *prove* it could be done, then fix the gap.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Industrial Meaning |
|---|---|
| **OT** | Operational Technology — hardware/software that monitors/controls physical processes |
| **ICS** | Industrial Control System — umbrella term for control networks |
| **SCADA** | Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition — centralized remote monitoring |
| **DCS** | Distributed Control System — localized controllers for plant subsystems |
| **PLC** | Programmable Logic Controller — ruggedized computer running ladder logic |
| **HMI** | Human-Machine Interface — operator dashboard |
| **Purdue Model** | 6-level reference architecture separating IT and OT networks |
| **TTPs** | Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (attacker behavior model) |
| **SIEM** | Security Information and Event Management system |
| **SOC** | Security Operations Center |
| **CISA** | Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (US) |
| **ISA/IEC 62443** | Global standard for industrial cybersecurity |

---

## 1.4 The Color Team Spectrum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Color Team Taxonomy (Industry Standard)**
>
> - **Red Team** → Offensive attackers (find weaknesses)
> - **Blue Team** → Defensive defenders (detect and respond)
> - **Purple Team** → Bridge / facilitator (maximize learning from red vs blue interaction)
> - **Yellow Team** → Software builders / developers
> - **Orange Team** → DevSecOps / secure-by-design advocates
> - **Green Team** → Awareness, training, cultural change agents

In industrial settings, the **red and purple** functions are the most operationally critical because a failed test in OT can cause **physical safety incidents**, not just data loss.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Red Teaming — The Adversarial Simulation Lifecycle

A formal red team engagement in an industrial setting follows a documented methodology, typically aligned with **NIST SP 800-115**, **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS**, and **CISA's Assessment Methodologies**.

### Step 1 — Engagement Scoping & Rules of Engagement (RoE)
- Define the **target environment** (Purdue Levels 0–5, see diagram).
- Identify **out-of-scope assets** (e.g., a live reactor that cannot tolerate scan traffic).
- Set **safety constraints**: no packet flooding, no PLC firmware uploads without approval.
- Authorize the team with a written "**Get-Out-of-Jail-Free**" letter signed by plant management.

### Step 2 — Threat Intelligence & Adversary Emulation
- Map engagement to a **named threat actor** (e.g., APT33 targeting energy sector; TRITON/Trisis malware).
- Select relevant **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS techniques**:
  - **T0830** — Adversary-in-the-Middle (L2 protocol abuse)
  - **T0836** — Modify Parameter (tamper PLC setpoints)
  - **T0855** — Unauthorized Command Message
  - **T0815** — Denial of View (blind the operators)
  - **T0832** — Manipulation of View (display fake readings on HMI)

### Step 3 — Reconnaissance (Passive & Active)
- **Passive**: OSINT, Shodan, vendor manuals, exposed engineering workstations.
- **Active**: Modbus/TCP and DNP3 enumeration, Siemens S7Comm discovery, EtherNet/IP scans.

### Step 4 — Initial Access
- Spear-phishing plant engineers (IT → OT pivot).
- Exploiting exposed engineering VPN gateways.
- Abusing **vendor remote access** (e.g., TeamViewer, VNC) on HMI stations.
- Using **removable media** dropped in parking lots (USB drop attacks — common in ICS).

### Step 5 — Lateral Movement (IT → OT Pivot)
- Compromise an engineering workstation in the corporate IT network.
- Use harvested credentials to access the **Industrial DMZ (IDMZ)**.
- Cross the **Purdue Level 3.5 boundary** (firewall between IT and OT).
- Reach a **Historian server** (e.g., OSIsoft PI) and pivot to a PLC subnet.

### Step 6 — Privilege Escalation & Persistence
- Crack PLC passwords (default vendor credentials).
- Implant a **logic bomb** in ladder logic (illustrative, not executed in real production).
- Establish a foothold in the **Engineering Workstation** for re-entry.

### Step 7 — Impact Simulation (Without Actual Damage)
- Demonstrate ability to **manipulate a setpoint** (e.g., raise reactor temperature setpoint from 80°C to 200°C).
- Demonstrate ability to **blind the HMI** (T0815 — Denial of View).
- Generate a **red team report** with the kill-chain map and remediation guidance.

## 2.2 Purple Teaming — The Collaboration Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Purple Teaming in OT?**
>
> Traditional red team → report → fix cycles in OT environments can take **months** because downtime windows are scarce and engineering changes to PLC logic require rigorous safety review. Purple teaming compresses this feedback loop by injecting **continuous, joint learning** between attackers and defenders.

### The Purple Team Cycle

1. **Plan** — Joint selection of TTPs (e.g., "simulate TRITON-style Safety Instrumented System attack").
2. **Execute** — Red team runs the attack in a controlled window.
3. **Observe** — Blue team monitors SIEM, EDR, and OT network sensors.
4. **Debrief** — Joint review of what was detected, missed, and delayed.
5. **Harden** — Blue team writes new detection rules (Sigma, Snort, Suricata for OT protocols).
6. **Retest** — Red team runs the same attack again to validate detection.
7. **Repeat** — Mature purple teams operate this as a **continuous exercise loop**.

### Purple Team Enablement Tools
- **MITRE Caldera** — Automated adversary emulation for OT.
- **Atomic Red Team** — Library of small, single-technique tests.
- **Red Canary's Atomic Test Library** — Mapping each test to ATT&CK.
- **Prelude Operator** — Community adversary emulation platform.
- **Grafana + OT telemetry dashboards** — Visualize detection coverage.

## 2.3 Industrial-Specific Constraints

| Constraint | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Safety** | A red team action must NEVER trigger unsafe physical state (e.g., opening a relief valve, over-pressurizing a tank). |
| **Availability** | OT processes run 24×7×365; tests must use maintenance windows or **digital twin / sandbox replicas**. |
| **Legacy protocols** | Modbus, DNP3, Profinet have **no built-in authentication** — testing reveals design flaws, not configuration issues. |
| **Vendor lockdowns** | PLC firmware is often cryptographically signed; red team must demonstrate without bricking equipment. |
| **Regulatory** | NERC-CIP (North American grid), IEC 62443, NIS2 (EU) mandate regular security assessments. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are used by Security Operations Centers (SOCs) and OT security teams to measure the effectiveness of red/purple team exercises.

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{\text{detect},i} - T_{\text{occur},i})
$$

Where:
- $MTTD$ = Mean Time To Detect
- $T_{\text{detect},i}$ = Time of detection for incident $i$
- $T_{\text{occur},i}$ = Time the malicious event began

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{\text{resolve},i} - T_{\text{detect},i})
$$

Where $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Respond (or Resolve).

$$
\text{MTTC} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{\text{contain},i} - T_{\text{detect},i})
$$

Where $MTTC$ = Mean Time To Contain.

$$
\text{Detection Coverage (\%)} = \frac{\vert \text{Detected TTPs} \vert}{\vert \text{Executed TTPs} \vert} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Detection Latency (s)} = T_{\text{SIEM alert}} - T_{\text{event}}
$$

$$
\text{Risk Score (CVSS v3.1)} = \text{round}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability}, 10))
$$

With $\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times \text{ISC}$ and $\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times \text{AV} \times \text{AC} \times \text{PR} \times \text{UI}$.

$$
\text{Attack Path Risk} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P(\text{step}_i) \times I(\text{step}_i)
$$

Where $P(\text{step}_i)$ = probability of success at step $i$, and $I(\text{step}_i)$ = impact if step succeeds.

$$
\text{Purple Team KPI} = \frac{\text{TTPs Detected + Validated}}{\text{TTPs Executed}} \times \frac{1}{\text{MTTD (hours)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: Students should remember that in OT environments, a single **unmitigated high-impact TTP** (e.g., setpoint manipulation) outweighs dozens of low-impact findings. The *severity-weighted* detection coverage is more meaningful than raw percentage.

### Metric Summary Table (Markdown-Safe, No Pipes)

| Metric | Unit | Target in OT SOC | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| MTTD | minutes/hours | less than 5 min for critical | Detection engineering focus |
| MTTR | minutes/hours | less than 30 min | Response runbook focus |
| MTTC | minutes/hours | less than 15 min | Containment focus |
| Detection Coverage | percent | greater than 90 percent for critical TTPs | ATT&CK-based measure |
| Detection Latency | seconds | less than 60 s | SIEM tuning KPI |
