| CVSS Base Score | 0 to 10 | prioritize greater than 7.0 | Asset risk triage |
| Attack Path Risk | dimensionless | minimize | Sum across path steps |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy sector**: Red teams validate whether an attacker could trigger an **uncontrolled generator trip** in a power plant.
- **Manufacturing**: Purple teams develop **Sigma rules** for detecting abnormal ladder-logic uploads to PLCs.
- **Water utilities**: Test whether an adversary could **change chlorine dosing setpoints** (a public-health threat).
- **Smart factories (Industry 4.0)**: Validate segmentation between **IIoT sensor mesh** and the **DCS control plane**.
- **Compliance evidence**: NERC-CIP-008, IEC 62443-2-4, and NIST CSF *DE.DP-3* explicitly require periodic red/purple team exercises as proof of due diligence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Detection Coverage with Worked Example

**Problem Context:** A purple team in an oil refinery executed 20 ATT&CK for ICS techniques during a quarterly exercise. The blue team correctly detected and alerted on 14 of them. Compute the Detection Coverage.

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

$$
\text{Step 1: Identify the formula.}
$$

$$
\text{Detection Coverage (\%)} = \frac{\vert \text{Detected TTPs} \vert}{\vert \text{Executed TTPs} \vert} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Substitute known values.}
$$

$$
\text{Detection Coverage (\%)} = \frac{14}{20} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Perform division first (order of operations).}
$$

$$
\frac{14}{20} = 0.7
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Multiply by 100 to convert to percentage.}
$$

$$
0.7 \times 100 = 70\%
$$

$$
\text{Therefore, Detection Coverage} = 70\%
$$

### Interpretation
The refinery's SOC detected **70%** of the simulated adversary techniques. Industry maturity targets in OT are:
- **Tier 1 (Initial)**: 40–60%
- **Tier 2 (Defined)**: 60–80%
- **Tier 3 (Advanced)**: 80–95%
- **Tier 4 (Optimized)**: greater than 95%

The refinery is at **Tier 2**, with significant room for detection engineering.

## 3.2 Derivation of MTTD — Worked Example

**Problem Context:** During a purple team drill, three malicious events occurred. Data:

| Incident | Event Occurred | SIEM Alert | Response Resolved |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10:00:00 | 10:08:00 | 10:35:00 |
| 2 | 11:15:00 | 11:18:00 | 11:50:00 |
| 3 | 14:30:00 | 14:31:00 | 14:55:00 |

### Step-by-Step Calculation of MTTD

$$
\text{Step 1: Compute per-incident detection deltas.}
$$

$$
\Delta t_{\text{detect},1} = 10{:}08{:}00 - 10{:}00{:}00 = 8 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\Delta t_{\text{detect},2} = 11{:}18{:}00 - 11{:}15{:}00 = 3 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\Delta t_{\text{detect},3} = 14{:}31{:}00 - 14{:}30{:}00 = 1 \text{ minute}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Apply the MTTD formula.}
$$

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{\text{detect},i} - T_{\text{occur},i})
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: Substitute.}
$$

$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{1}{3} (8 + 3 + 1)
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: Sum.}
$$

$$
8 + 3 + 1 = 12 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{Step 5: Divide.}
$$

$$
\frac{12}{3} = 4 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{MTTD} = 4 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Interpretation
A MTTD of **4 minutes** is excellent for an OT SOC (target less than 5 minutes for critical TTPs). The downward trend (8 → 3 → 1 min) shows the purple team feedback loop is *compressing* detection latency exercise-over-exercise.

## 3.3 Derivation of MTTR (Same Data Set)

$$
\text{Step 1: Compute per-incident response deltas.}
$$

$$
\Delta t_{\text{resp},1} = 10{:}35{:}00 - 10{:}08{:}00 = 27 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\Delta t_{\text{resp},2} = 11{:}50{:}00 - 11{:}18{:}00 = 32 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\Delta t_{\text{resp},3} = 14{:}55{:}00 - 14{:}31{:}00 = 24 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: Sum and divide by } N.
$$

$$
27 + 32 + 24 = 83 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{83}{3} = 27.67 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\therefore \text{MTTR} \approx 27.7 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Interpretation
Target MTTR in OT is typically less than 30 minutes for critical asset compromise. The team is **just under threshold** — purple team debrief should focus on shortening containment time.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Red vs Blue vs Purple (Industrial)

| Dimension | Red Team | Blue Team | Purple Team |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Discover exploitable weaknesses | Detect & respond to attacks | Joint learning & validation |
| Operator Mindset | Adversary (APT emulation) | Defender (SOC analyst) | Coach / Facilitator |
| Output | Adversary emulation report, kill-chain map | Detection rules, runbooks, IR reports | Continuous detection-coverage dashboard |
| Cadence in OT | Quarterly / annual / post-incident | 24×7 monitoring | Sprint-based (2-week cycles) |
| Tooling | C2 frameworks, OT protocol fuzzers, exploit kits | SIEM, EDR, NDR, OT sensors | MITRE Caldera, Atomic Red Team, detection content repos |
| Risk to Operations | Medium (must be scoped) | Low | Low–Medium |
| Regulatory Mapping | NERC-CIP-008, IEC 62443-2-4 | NERC-CIP-005, -007 | NIST CSF DE.DP-3, ISO 27001 A.12.6 |
| Success Metric | Number of undetected TTPs | MTTD / MTTR | Trend in Detection Coverage % |
| Engagement Mode | Adversarial | Reactive | Cooperative |

## 3.5 MITRE ATT&CK for ICS — High-Yield Technique List (KTU Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following eight ICS-specific techniques — they appear frequently in KTU exam questions and are the *most commonly emulated* by red teams in industrial engagements.

| Technique ID | Name | Industrial Impact |
|---|---|---|
| T0830 | Adversary-in-the-Middle | Intercepts L2/L3 OT traffic, e.g., Modbus/TCP poisoning |
| T0836 | Modify Parameter | Tampering of PLC/DCS setpoints (temperature, pressure, flow) |
| T0855 | Unauthorized Command Message | Forged control commands to field devices |
| T0858 | Change Operating Mode | Puts PLC into stop or program mode (DoS) |
| T0815 | Denial of View | Blinds HMI by interrupting historian reporting |
| T0832 | Manipulation of View | Displays false readings to operators |
| T0807 | Command-Line Interface | Execution of native OT utilities on engineering workstation |
| T0866 | Exploitation of Remote Services | Pivoting via exposed vendor remote access (RDP, VNC, TeamViewer) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Purdue Model — Attack Path for Red Team

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["Purdue Level 5 - Enterprise Network"]
    L4["Purdue Level 4 - Site Business Planning"]
    L3["Purdue Level 3.5 - Industrial DMZ"]
    L3b["Purdue Level 3 - Site Operations"]
    L2["Purdue Level 2 - Supervisory Control - SCADA HMI"]
    L1["Purdue Level 1 - Basic Control - PLC DCS"]
    L0["Purdue Level 0 - Physical Process - Sensors Actuators"]

    L5 -->|"spear phishing credential harvest"| L4
    L4 -->|"lateral move via SMB RDP"| L3
    L3 -->|"firewall traversal IDMZ"| L3b
    L3b -->|"Modbus DNP3 enumeration"| L2
    L2 -->|"engineering workstation compromise"| L1
    L1 -->|"setpoint manipulation Modify Parameter"| L0

    style L3 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px
    style L3b fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style L1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style L0 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Reading Guide:** The red color intensity increases from Level 3.5 downward, indicating the increasing **impact severity** as the attacker approaches the physical process. Level 3.5 (the **Industrial DMZ**) is the most critical chokepoint — it is where the IT/OT boundary firewall resides.

## 4.2 Purple Team Continuous Improvement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Plan Joint TTP Selection"]
    B["Red Team Executes Attack"]
    C["Blue Team Monitors SIEM OT Sensors"]
    D["Joint Debrief Compare Logs"]
    E["Blue Team Writes Detection Content"]
    F["Red Team Retests Same TTP"]
    G["Update Detection Coverage Dashboard"]
    H["Plan Next Sprint Cycle"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> A

    style A fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style C fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFFFFF
    style F fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style G fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,color:#FFFFFF
    style H fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Reading Guide:** Purple (joint), red (offensive), and blue (defensive) nodes alternate through each cycle. The output of every iteration is an updated **Detection Coverage Dashboard**, which serves as the single source of truth for the SOC's maturity.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Red & Purple Team in an Industrial SOC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["Threat Intelligence Plane"]
        A1["MITRE ATT&CK for ICS"]
        A2["Adversary Playbooks APT33 TRITON"]
        A3["OSINT Vendor Advisories"]
    end

    subgraph B["Offensive Plane - Red Team"]
        B1["C2 Framework - Empire - Cobalt Strike"]
        B2["OT Protocol Fuzzers - Modbus - DNP3"]
        B3["Exploit Kit Metasploit ICS Modules"]
        B4["Adversary Emulation - Caldera"]
    end

    subgraph C["Defensive Plane - Blue Team"]
        C1["SIEM Splunk - QRadar"]
        C2["OT Sensors - Nozomi - Claroty"]
        C3["EDR - CrowdStrike"]
        C4["IDS - Suricata OT Rules"]
    end

    subgraph D["Collaboration Plane - Purple Team"]
        D1["Joint Slack War Room"]
        D2["Sigma Rule Repo"]
        D3["Detection Coverage Dashboard"]
        D4["Maturity Scoring - CIS Controls"]
    end

    subgraph E["Industrial Process Plane"]
        E1["Purdue Level 0 - Physical"]
        E2["Purdue Level 1 - PLC DCS"]
        E3["Purdue Level 2 - SCADA HMI"]
        E4["Purdue Level 3 - Engineering"]
    end

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B -->|"simulated TTPs"| E
    E -->|"telemetry logs"| C
    C -->|"alerts raw events"| D
    B -->|"attack narrative"| D
    D -->|"new detection rules"| C
    D -->|"coaching feedback"| B

    style A fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style C fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B
    style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
```

**Reading Guide:** This 5-plane architecture shows how red and purple teaming operate inside an industrial SOC. The **Threat Intelligence Plane** feeds both attack emulation (red) and detection engineering (blue). The **Collaboration Plane** (purple) is the feedback bus — every TTP executed by the red team generates data that flows through the collaboration plane to improve blue team defenses, and every new detection rule is validated by the red team re-testing.

## 4.4 Engagement Decision Tree — Red vs Purple Team Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the goal to find unknown weaknesses?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2{"Is downtime acceptable for independent adversarial testing?"}
    Q1 -->|"No - goal is to validate existing detections"| PURPLE["Choose PURPLE TEAM"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes - plant offline or sandbox twin"| RED["Choose RED TEAM"]
    Q2 -->|"No - production OT live"| Q3{"Is there a mature blue team in place?"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes - SOC analysts available"| PURPLE2["Choose PURPLE TEAM"]
    Q3 -->|"No - immature SOC"| RED2["Choose RED TEAM with coaching handover"]
    RED --> REPORT["Final Output Adversary Report"]
    PURPLE --> DASH["Final Output Detection Coverage Dashboard"]
    RED2 --> HYBRID["Hybrid Red-Purple Exercise"]
    PURPLE2 --> DASH

    style RED fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style PURPLE fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style PURPLE2 fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style RED2 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style HYBRID fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style REPORT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style DASH fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`** Define **Red Teaming** in the context of industrial control systems. Mention any two ATT&CK for ICS techniques that a red team might emulate.

**Model Answer:**
Red teaming in industrial control systems is a goal-oriented, full-scope adversarial simulation in which a designated offensive team emulates the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) of real-world threat actors against the organization's IT and OT (Operational Technology) environment, **without prior insider knowledge of defensive controls**, to identify exploitable weaknesses.

Two ATT&CK for ICS techniques commonly emulated are:
1. **T0836 — Modify Parameter**: The red team demonstrates the ability to tamper with a PLC setpoint (e.g., temperature, pressure), proving that an attacker could cause unsafe physical state.
2. **T0815 — Denial of View**: The red team demonstrates the ability to blind the operators by interrupting historian-to-HMI communication, simulating what an attacker would do prior to a destructive action.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Differentiate between **Red Teaming** and **Purple Teaming** with respect to objectives, team composition, and output deliverables.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Red Teaming | Purple Teaming |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Discover unknown weaknesses via adversarial emulation | Maximize detection/response learning via joint cooperation |
| Team Composition | External or independent offensive operators | Joint red + blue operators working in tandem |
| Output Deliverable | Adversary emulation report (kill-chain map, findings) | Detection-coverage dashboard, validated Sigma/Suricata rules, maturity scorecard |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS framework** in detail. Explain any **six** techniques relevant to industrial red team engagements with examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Purple Team continuous improvement cycle** with a neat block diagram. Describe how this model improves SOC maturity in an industrial setting. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — Part (a)
The **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** framework is a curated knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques specifically observed in attacks against industrial control systems. It is organized into **11 tactics** (initial access, execution, persistence, privilege escalation, evasion, discovery, lateral movement, collection, command-and-control, inhibition of response, impairment of control, damage to property) and dozens of techniques.

**Six techniques with examples:**

1. **T0830 — Adversary-in-the-Middle (MitM):**
The attacker intercepts and relays L2/L3 traffic between, e.g., a Modbus/TCP master and a PLC. Example: ARP-spoofing on a Level 2 cell to inject false sensor readings to the PLC.

2. **T0836 — Modify Parameter:**
The attacker alters control-loop setpoints (temperature, pressure, flow rate). Example: Increasing a furnace's temperature setpoint from 80°C to 200°C in a steel plant.

3. **T0855 — Unauthorized Command Message:**
The attacker sends forged control commands to a field device. Example: Triggering an emergency stop on a conveyor line that was not authorized by the operator.

4. **T0858 — Change Operating Mode:**
The attacker shifts a PLC from RUN mode to PROGRAM or STOP mode. Example: Halting a bottling line's PLC, causing production loss (and potentially safety events).

5. **T0815 — Denial of View:**
The attacker disrupts historian-to-HMI data flow, blinding operators. Example: Dropping DNP3 traffic between the SCADA server and a substation HMI prior to a subversion attack.

6. **T0866 — Exploitation of Remote Services:**
The attacker exploits vendor remote-access solutions (RDP, VNC, TeamViewer). Example: Using default TeamViewer credentials on an engineering workstation to access the control network from the internet.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)
The **Purple Team continuous improvement cycle** is a structured, sprint-based methodology in which the red team and blue team operate jointly to maximize organizational learning from every engagement.

**Stages:**

1. **Plan**: Joint selection of TTPs from MITRE ATT&CK for ICS that align with the threat model (e.g., emulate TRITON-style SIS attack).
2. **Execute**: Red team executes the TTP in a controlled test window.
3. **Observe**: Blue team monitors SIEM, EDR, and OT sensors, capturing detection success/failure.
4. **Debrief**: Joint review of detection logs, identifying blind spots and latency.
5. **Harden**: Blue team authors new Sigma rules, Suricata signatures, or OT-specific playbooks.
6. **Retest**: Red team re-executes the same TTP to validate the new detection.
7. **Repeat**: Sprint cycle continues, tracked on a detection-coverage dashboard.

**Impact on SOC Maturity:**
- Compresses the feedback loop from *months* (in a traditional red → report → fix cycle) to *days*.
- Increases detection coverage incrementally — each sprint targets previously uncovered TTPs.
- Builds muscle memory for the SOC analysts in handling real incidents.
- Provides auditable evidence for **NERC-CIP-008**, **IEC 62443-2-4**, and **NIST CSF DE.DP-3** compliance.
- Reduces **MTTD** and **MTTR** quantitatively (measurable via dashboards).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A(b)**
> Many students draw a flowchart but **fail to explain how each stage quantitatively improves SOC maturity**. A 14-mark answer must *map* each stage to a measurable KPI (Detection Coverage %, MTTD, MTTR) — a diagram without this mapping will cap your marks at 4/7 for part (b).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Compare **Red Team, Blue Team, and Purple Team** engagements across nine dimensions. Justify why purple teaming is preferred for OT environments. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing SOC executes a purple team drill. **15** adversary techniques are executed. The blue team detects **12**. The total time spent by the red team across the drill is **6 hours**, and the SOC's mean detection latency is **90 seconds**. Compute:
(i) **Detection Coverage (%)**
(ii) **Purple Team KPI** using the formula $\text{KPI} = \dfrac{\text{Detection Coverage (\%)}}{\text{MTTD (hours)}}$ where MTTD is derived from a 90-second latency.
*(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

| Dimension | Red Team | Blue Team | Purple Team |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Goal | Discover weaknesses | Detect and respond | Joint learning |
| 2. Mindset | Adversary | Defender | Coach |
| 3. Cadence | Quarterly / Annual | 24×7 | Sprint-based (2 weeks) |
| 4. Tooling | C2, OT fuzzers | SIEM, EDR, OT sensors | Caldera, Atomic Red Team |
| 5. Output | Adversary report | IR report, runbooks | Coverage dashboard |
| 6. Team Comp | Independent operators | SOC analysts | Joint red + blue |
| 7. Risk to Plant | Medium (scoped) | Low | Low–Medium |
| 8. Regulatory Mapping | NERC-CIP-008 | NERC-CIP-005, -007 | NIST CSF DE.DP-3 |
| 9. Success Metric | Undetected TTPs | MTTD/MTTR | Detection Coverage % trend |

**Why purple teaming is preferred for OT:**
1. **Safety-critical operations**: Production downtime is scarce; purple teaming uses short, controlled sprints instead of long red-team engagements.
2. **Compressed feedback loop**: Detection engineering happens *during* the exercise, not months later.
3. **Continuous compliance**: Regulatory frameworks (NERC-CIP, IEC 62443) require ongoing validation — purple teams generate continuous evidence.
4. **Skill development**: SOC analysts gain hands-on attacker perspective without requiring a dedicated red team budget.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**(i) Detection Coverage**

$$
\text{Detection Coverage (\%)} = \frac{\vert \text{Detected TTPs} \vert}{\vert \text{Executed TTPs} \vert} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Detection Coverage} = \frac{12}{15} \times 100
$$

$$
= 0.8 \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**(ii) Purple Team KPI**

First, convert MTTD to hours:

$$
\text{MTTD} = 90 \text{ seconds} = \frac{90}{3600} \text{ hours} = 0.025 \text{ hours}
$$

Then compute the KPI:

$$
\text{KPI} = \frac{\text{Detection Coverage (\%)}}{\text{MTTD (hours)}}
$$

$$
= \frac{80}{0.025}
$$

$$
= 3200
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Detection Coverage} = 80\%, \quad \text{Purple Team KPI} = 3200}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(b)**
> The most common mistake students make is **forgetting to convert seconds to hours** before plugging MTTD into the KPI formula. If you substitute 90 (seconds) directly, you get a wildly inflated KPI = 0.89, which is dimensionally meaningless. Always normalize units *first* (90 s = 0.025 h), then compute. This unit-normalization step is the differentiator between 5/7 and 7/7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this before your KTU exam)**

- [x] **Red Teaming** = Adversarial simulation of real attackers; goal is to find weaknesses.
- [x] **Purple Teaming** = Joint red + blue collaboration; goal is to maximize learning.
- [x] **OT environments** add physical-safety, availability, and legacy-protocol constraints absent in IT.
- [x] **Purdue Model** has 6 levels (0–5); Level 3.5 (Industrial DMZ) is the IT/OT chokepoint.
- [x] **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** is the de-facto taxonomy; memorize at least T0830, T0836, T0855, T0858, T0815, T0832, T0807, T0866.
- [x] **MTTD** (Mean Time To Detect) is the primary SOC KPI for purple team maturity.
- [x] **MTTR** (Mean Time To Respond) and **MTTC** (Mean Time To Contain) measure response effectiveness.
- [x] **Detection Coverage (%)** = (Detected TTPs / Executed TTPs) × 100.
- [x] **Purple Team KPI** = Detection Coverage (%) / MTTD (hours) — always normalize units first.
- [x] **Sigma rules** and **Suricata OT signatures** are the typical detection-engineering artifacts produced by purple teams.
- [x] **Tools to remember**: MITRE Caldera, Atomic Red Team, Prelude Operator, Nozomi, Claroty, Suricata with OT rulesets.
- [x] **Compliance frameworks**: NERC-CIP-008 (incident response), IEC 62443-2-4 (security program), NIST CSF DE.DP-3 (testing).
- [x] **Never** run a red team TTP that could cause physical damage without a sandboxed digital twin.
- [x] **Purple team sprints** run on 2-week cycles; traditional red team engagements are quarterly/annual.
- [x] The **kill chain** in OT is: IT compromise → lateral move → IDMZ traversal → Level 3 access → Level 2/1 pivot → setpoint manipulation.
- [x] **Unit conversions**: 60 s = 1 min, 3600 s = 1 h, 86,400 s = 1 day — always normalize before substituting into KPI formulas.
- [x] Always carry **box your final numerical answer** with the unit to earn full marks on KTU ESE Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security metrics and reporting in OT: KPIs for OT security operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT746 - SECURITY OPERATIONS ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security metrics and reporting in OT: KPIs for OT security operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Security Metrics in OT (Operational Technology):** Quantifiable measures used to evaluate the effectiveness, efficiency, and maturity of cybersecurity controls protecting Industrial Control Systems (ICS), SCADA, DCS, and PLC-based environments. Unlike IT metrics that prioritize the **CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)**, OT metrics prioritize the **AIC triad (Availability, Integrity, Confidentiality)** and add the **Safety** dimension as the paramount concern.

**Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for OT Security Operations** are a curated subset of security metrics that are:
- **Actionable** (drive decisions)
- **Business-aligned** (linked to plant uptime and safety)
- **Time-bound** (measured over defined intervals)
- **Comparative** (benchmarked against baselines)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hospital ICU Analogy:**
> Imagine an Industrial Plant as a **Hospital Intensive Care Unit (ICU)**. The patients (production processes) cannot be "rebooted" without risking death (catastrophic physical failure). 
> - **IT security metrics** are like measuring the *security of hospital administrative computers* (patient records, billing).
> - **OT security metrics** are like measuring the *security of life-support machines, ventilators, and infusion pumps*.
> 
> In the ICU, you don't ask "How fast did the alarm sound?" — you ask "Did the patient survive? Was the oxygen flow uninterrupted? Did the dosage remain accurate?" — these are **OT-specific KPIs** where **Safety and Availability trump everything else**.

## 1.3 Why Traditional IT Metrics Fail in OT

| IT-Focused Metric | Why It Fails in OT |
|---|---|
| **Mean Time to Patch (MTTP)** | Patches in OT often require plant shutdowns scheduled months in advance |
| **Antivirus Coverage %** | Active scanning can crash legacy PLCs and HMIs |
| **Phishing Click Rate** | Field operators may have air-gapped, non-email workstations |
| **Data Exfiltration Volume** | An attacker can cause physical damage with **tiny** data packets (e.g., setpoint changes) |
| **Encryption Coverage** | Latency from encryption can break real-time control loops (e.g., Modbus/TCP needs < 100 ms response) |

## 1.4 The OT Metrics Hierarchy (Three-Tier Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 1 — Strategic Metrics:** Reported to executives and plant managers (e.g., Annual Loss Expectancy, Safety Incident Rate).
> 
> **Tier 2 — Operational Metrics:** Reported to SOC managers and OT engineers (e.g., MTTD, MTTR, Patch Compliance).
> 
> **Tier 3 — Tactical/Technical Metrics:** Used by analysts daily (e.g., Number of unauthorized PLC program uploads, Failed login attempts on HMI).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind OT Metrics

Traditional IT security is **reactive** (detect → respond → recover). OT security must be **proactive + safety-first**. Metrics serve three engineering purposes:
1. **Quantify Risk** — Convert "we might be vulnerable" into "Asset X has a CVSS of 9.1 with 3 known exploits".
2. **Validate Controls** — Prove that firewalls, DMZs, and jump servers are actually working.
3. **Drive Continuous Improvement** — Detect drift (e.g., new unauthorized device added to the network).

## 2.2 The Core Categories of OT Security KPIs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **6 core KPI categories** (per IEC 62443-2-4 and NIST SP 800-82r3):

### Category 1: Incident Response KPIs
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD):** Average time from incident occurrence to detection.
- **Mean Time to Respond/Contain (MTTC):** Average time from detection to containment.
- **Mean Time to Recover (MTTR):** Average time from containment to full restoration.
- **First-Time Resolution Rate (FTRR):** % of incidents resolved without escalation.

### Category 2: Vulnerability Management KPIs
- **Mean Time to Patch (MTTP):** Average time from CVE disclosure to patch deployment.
- **Vulnerability Density:** Vulnerabilities per asset (per Purdue Level).
- **Patch Compliance Rate:** % of assets patched within SLA.
- **Critical CVE Remediation Rate:** % of CVSS ≥ 7.0 vulnerabilities closed within 30 days.

### Category 3: Network Visibility & Segmentation KPIs
- **Asset Visibility Coverage:** % of OT assets visible in the SOC's inventory (CMDB).
- **Network Segmentation Effectiveness:** % of traffic between Purdue Levels denied by default.
- **Unauthorized Device Rate:** # of rogue devices detected per 1000 assets per month.
- **East-West Traffic Anomalies:** # of anomalous internal zone crossings per week.

### Category 4: Operational Continuity KPIs
- **OT System Availability %:** Uptime of control systems (target: 99.95% for safety-critical).
- **Process Integrity Index:** % of control commands validated against expected range.
- **Safety System Availability:** % of SIS (Safety Instrumented Systems) in operational state.
- **Unplanned Downtime due to Cyber:** Hours of unplanned shutdown from cyber events.

### Category 5: Compliance & Governance KPIs
- **Policy Compliance %:** Adherence to IEC 62443, NERC-CIP, NIST CSF.
- **Audit Finding Closure Rate:** # of audit findings closed / total findings.
- **Training Completion Rate:** % of OT staff with current security certification.
- **Access Review Frequency:** # of access reviews per year (quarterly is best practice).

### Category 6: Threat Intelligence KPIs
- **Threat Intel Actionability Rate:** % of intel reports translated into detection rules.
- **IOC Hit Rate:** # of confirmed IOCs / total IOCs deployed.
- **Threat Hunt Findings:** # of previously undetected threats found via proactive hunting.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Industry Target (OT)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| MTTD | $\text{MTTD} = \frac{\sum (t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{occur}})}{N_{\text{incidents}}}$ | Hours | < 24 hrs |
| MTTC | $\text{MTTC} = \frac{\sum (t_{\text{contain}} - t_{\text{detect}})}{N_{\text{incidents}}}$ | Hours | < 4 hrs |
| MTTR | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum (t_{\text{recover}} - t_{\text{contain}})}{N_{\text{incidents}}}$ | Hours | < 72 hrs |
| Availability % | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}} \times 100$ | % | 99.95% |
| Patch Compliance % | $P_c = \frac{N_{\text{patched}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | ≥ 95% |
| Asset Visibility % | $V = \frac{N_{\text{discovered}}}{N_{\text{expected}}} \times 100$ | % | ≥ 98% |
| Critical CVE Closure Rate | $R_{cve} = \frac{N_{\text{closed}}}{N_{\text{critical}}} \times 100$ | % | 100% in 30 days |
| FTRR % | $\text{FTRR} = \frac{N_{\text{resolved\_L1}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | ≥ 80% |
| Risk Score (Asset) | $R_{\text{asset}} = T \times V \times C$ | Score 1-125 | Minimize |
| Annual Loss Expectancy | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Currency (₹/$) | Minimize |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures
> - **MDT** = Mean Downtime
> - **T** = Threat likelihood, **V** = Vulnerability score, **C** = Consequence/impact
> - **SLE** = Single Loss Expectancy, **ARO** = Annualized Rate of Occurrence

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Power Utilities** use **Safety System Availability** as a regulatory KPI (NERC-CIP-008).
- **Oil & Gas** plants track **Unplanned Cyber Downtime** to satisfy insurance carriers.
- **Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** uses **Asset Visibility %** to measure IIoT device governance.
- **Pharmaceutical** firms use **Integrity Index** to satisfy FDA 21 CFR Part 11 audit trails.
- **Water Treatment** plants use **OT MTTR** as a Tier-1 KPI for the mayor's public dashboard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: System Availability Formula (Engineering Rigor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The OT community universally uses the **Reliability Engineering** definition of availability, derived from MTBF and MDT. Below is the full derivation, since KTU 2024 expects B.Tech students to **derive** this equation, not just memorize it.

### Step 1: Define the Operational Cycle
Consider an OT asset (e.g., a PLC) over a long observation window $T_{\text{obs}}$. The system alternates between two states:
- **Up state** (operating correctly)
- **Down state** (failed, under maintenance, or compromised)

### Step 2: Express Total Uptime
Let $N$ be the number of failures observed. The total operating time is:
$$
T_{\text{up}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{MTBF}_i
$$

### Step 3: Express Total Downtime
The total downtime (maintenance + recovery from cyber incidents):
$$
T_{\text{down}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{MDT}_i
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Law of Large Numbers
As $N \to \infty$, the average values converge:
$$
\overline{\text{MTBF}} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{MTBF}_i
$$

$$
\overline{\text{MDT}} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{MDT}_i
$$

### Step 5: Define Availability Ratio
Availability is the **fraction of time the system is operational**:
$$
A_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}} = \frac{N \cdot \overline{\text{MTBF}}}{N \cdot \overline{\text{MTBF}} + N \cdot \overline{\text{MDT}}}
$$

### Step 6: Simplify
The factor $N$ cancels from numerator and denominator:
$$
A_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{\overline{\text{MTBF}}}{\overline{\text{MTBF}} + \overline{\text{MDT}}}
$$

### Step 7: Convert to Percentage
$$
A_{\%} = \frac{\overline{\text{MTBF}}}{\overline{\text{MTBF}} + \overline{\text{MDT}}} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a **safety-critical** SIS (Safety Instrumented System) in a refinery, the required SIL-3 availability is **99.99%**, meaning the system can be down only **52.6 minutes per year**. This is why OT security metrics emphasize **prevention over response** — you cannot tolerate the downtime that comes with reactive cybersecurity.

## 3.2 Derivation: Quantitative Risk Score (NIST SP 800-30 / IEC 62443-3-3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The risk score is **multiplicative**, not additive. A high vulnerability with no threat exposure has lower risk than a moderate vulnerability actively being exploited.

### Step 1: Define the Component Variables
- **T** = Threat Likelihood (scale 1-5: Very Low → Very High)
- **V** = Vulnerability Severity (scale 1-5)
- **C** = Consequence (scale 1-5: Negligible → Catastrophic)

### Step 2: Compute the Risk Magnitude
$$
R_{\text{score}} = T \times V \times C
$$

### Step 3: Map to Risk Tier
$$
\text{Risk Tier} = \begin{cases} \text{Critical} & \text{if } R_{\text{score}} \geq 75 \\ \text{High} & \text{if } 50 \le R_{\text{score}} < 75 \\ \text{Medium} & \text{if } 25 \le R_{\text{score}} < 50 \\ \text{Low} & \text{if } R_{\text{score}} < 25 \end{cases}
$$

### Step 4: Example Calculation
A refinery has a Modbus gateway (V = 5) with a known exploit, exposed to the internet (T = 4), where compromise could trigger an explosion (C = 5):
$$
R_{\text{score}} = 4 \times 5 \times 5 = 100 \implies \text{Critical}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping KPIs to Purdue Model Levels

| **Purdue Level** | **Primary Asset Types** | **Most Critical KPIs** | **Reporting Frequency** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** (Process) | Sensors, Actuators, Motors | Process Integrity Index, Safety System Availability | Real-time |
| **Level 1** (Basic Control) | PLCs, DCS Controllers, RTUs | Controller Firmware Compliance, Unauthorized Program Upload Rate | Weekly |
| **Level 2** (Supervisory) | HMIs, SCADA Servers, Engineering Workstations | Patch Compliance, MTTD, Access Review Frequency | Monthly |
| **Level 3** (Operations) | MES, Historians, Asset Management | OT-IT Traffic Anomalies, Vulnerability Density | Monthly |
| **Level 3.5** (DMZ) | Jump Servers, AV, Firewalls | MTTC, IOC Hit Rate, DMZ Bypass Attempts | Weekly |
| **Level 4/5** (Enterprise) | ERP, Email, Internet | FTRR, Training Completion Rate, Policy Compliance % | Quarterly |

## 3.4 Worked Example: MTTD Calculation (KTU Typical Problem)

**Problem:** A manufacturing plant logs 5 OT security incidents in Q1:
| Incident | Occurred (t₁) | Detected (t₂) | Time to Detect (hrs) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Jan 5, 02:00 | Jan 5, 14:00 | 12 |
| 2 | Jan 18, 06:00 | Jan 19, 18:00 | 36 |
| 3 | Feb 2, 10:00 | Feb 2, 22:00 | 12 |
| 4 | Feb 20, 00:00 | Feb 21, 12:00 | 36 |
| 5 | Mar 10, 08:00 | Mar 10, 08:30 | 0.5 |

**Solution:**
$$
\text{MTTD} = \frac{12 + 36 + 12 + 36 + 0.5}{5} = \frac{96.5}{5} = 19.3 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The plant exceeds the 24-hour industry target. Root cause: incidents during off-hours (02:00, 06:00, 00:00) took 36 hours to detect — indicating **no 24/7 SOC monitoring**. Recommendation: deploy passive OT IDS at Purdue Level 1.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OT Security Metrics Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify OT Assets and Zones] --> B[Map Assets to Purdue Levels]
    B --> C[Select KPIs from 6 Categories]
    C --> D[Define Baselines and Targets]
    D --> E[Collect Data via OT Sensors and SIEM]
    E --> F[Calculate Metrics MTTD MTTR MTTP]
    F --> G{Does Metric Exceed Threshold?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Trigger Alert and Root Cause Analysis]
    H --> I[Update Control and KPI Definition]
    I --> D
    G -- No --> J[Generate Executive Dashboard Report]
    J --> K[Distribute to Plant CISO and OT Manager]
    K --> L[Quarterly Board Review]
    L --> M[Annual Maturity Reassessment]
    M --> A
```


## 4.3 OT Metric Categorization Hierarchy

## 4.4 Data Flow: From Sensor to Executive Dashboard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PLC1[PLC Level 0] --> SCADA1[SCADA Server Level 2]
    PLC2[PLC Level 0] --> SCADA1
    HMI1[HMI Workstation] --> SCADA1
    SCADA1 --> OTIDS[Passive OT IDS Level 3.5]
    OTIDS --> SIEM[OT SIEM with Asset DB]
    FW[Industrial Firewall Logs] --> SIEM
    AV[Engineering Workstation AV Logs] --> SIEM
    SIEM --> K1[KPI Engine MTTD MTTR]
    SIEM --> K2[KPI Engine Patch and Visibility]
    SIEM --> K3[KPI Engine Availability and Safety]
    K1 --> D1[SOC Dashboard Tactical]
```
